1.lecture:
§1 · Three rate laws, one job

Heat moves by exactly three physical mechanisms, and this course is organized around learning each one's rate law well enough to design against it. Conduction, Fourier's law, \(q=-kA\frac{dT}{dx}\): a rate set by a material property \(k\) (thermal conductivity), the area it acts through, and the steepness of the temperature gradient it opposes. Convection, Newton's law of cooling, \(q=hA(T_s-T_\infty)\): a rate set by a convection coefficient \(h\) that is not a material property at all — it is a lumped stand-in for an entire boundary-layer flow field that Lessons 4–6 exist to unpack. Radiation, \(q=\varepsilon\sigma A(T_s^4-T_{surr}^4)\): the only mode that needs no material medium whatsoever, and the only one that is fundamentally nonlinear in temperature rather than linear in a temperature difference.

§2 · The thermal resistance network

Despite their different physics, all three rate laws can be rearranged into the same shape — a driving temperature difference divided by a resistance: \(q=\Delta T/R\). For convection, \(R_{conv}=1/(hA)\), read directly off Newton's law. For conduction through a plane wall, \(R_{wall}=L/(kA)\). For conduction through a cylindrical shell (a pipe wall, a wrapped cable), \(R_{cyl}=\ln(r_2/r_1)/(2\pi k L)\) — a ratio of radii rather than a thickness, because the area available for conduction grows with radius, so a fixed radial increment does not carry a fixed resistance increment the way a fixed-thickness plane-wall increment does. Layers encountered in series carry the same heat flow through each one, so their resistances simply add: \(R_{tot}=\sum_i R_i\), exactly like resistors in a DC circuit, with temperature standing in for voltage and heat rate for current.

§3 · Insulation economics, and the critical-radius surprise

Adding insulation to a plane wall always adds resistance and always reduces heat loss — area is fixed, so more thickness is pure gain. Wrapping insulation around a small-diameter cylinder — a wire, a small instrument line — does two things at once: it adds conduction resistance (wanted) but it also adds surface area for the outer convection film (unwanted, since \(R_{conv}=1/(hA)\) falls as area rises). The total resistance as a function of wrap radius \(r\) has a genuine extremum at the critical radius \(r_{cr}=k_{ins}/h\): for a bare radius smaller than \(r_{cr}\), adding insulation up to \(r_{cr}\) can increase total heat loss before it starts decreasing it. A windier site (higher \(h\)) shrinks \(r_{cr}\) and shrinks the zone this trap can occur in; a bare conductor already thicker than \(r_{cr}\) never sees it — insulation helps it immediately, as expected.

§4 · A worked resistance chain, and a worked critical radius

A building envelope carries four layers in series, per square metre of wall: an outdoor film at \(h_{out}=25\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\), a 20 mm brick layer at \(k=0.72\ \text{W/mK}\), a 50 mm insulation layer at \(k=0.04\ \text{W/mK}\), and an indoor film at \(h_{in}=8\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\), between a 45 °C outdoor design day and a 24 °C indoor setpoint. The four resistances are \(R_1=0.040\), \(R_2=0.278\), \(R_3=1.250\), \(R_4=0.125\ \ \text{m}^2\text{K/W}\), summing to \(R_{tot}=1.693\ \text{m}^2\text{K/W}\) and a flux \(q''=\Delta T/R_{tot}=12.41\ \text{W/m}^2\) — the same 12.41 W/m² through every layer, stepping the temperature down 45 → 44.50 → 41.06 → 25.55 → 24.0 °C, the last figure closing the loop back to the indoor setpoint exactly. The insulation alone carries 74% of the total — the layer worth upgrading first. Separately: a cable insulated with \(k_{ins}=0.15\ \ \text{W/mK}\) material in \(h=15\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) still air has \(r_{cr}=k_{ins}/h=10\ \text{mm}\) — smaller than many real bare-cable radii, so a thin wrap on such a cable can genuinely raise its heat loss before enough insulation is added to lower it again.

Key relation — series thermal resistance

\[ R_{tot}=\sum_i R_i, \qquad q=\frac{\Delta T_{overall}}{R_{tot}} \]

Every later lesson's exchangers, fins, and radiation networks are this same sum, extended.

quiz.q:
The building-envelope resistance chain

Problem. An exterior wall carries, in series per m²: an outdoor film \(h_{out}=25\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\), a 20 mm brick layer (\(k=0.72\ \text{W/mK}\)), a 50 mm insulation layer (\(k=0.04\ \text{W/mK}\)), and an indoor film \(h_{in}=8\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\), between 45°C outdoors and 24°C indoors. Find each resistance, the total, and the heat flux.

Doubling the insulation thickness

Problem. For the same wall, the insulation is upgraded from 50 mm to 100 mm (same \(k=0.04\ \text{W/mK}\), all else unchanged). Find the new total resistance and heat flux, and the percent reduction in q''.
quiz.solution: Solution. \(R_3'=0.1/0.04=2.500\ \text{m}^2\text{K/W}\), replacing \(R_3=1.250\). \(R_{tot}'=0.040+0.278+2.500+0.125=2.943\ \text{m}^2\text{K/W}\). \(q''=(45-24)/2.943=7.14\ \text{W/m}^2\), down from 12.41 — a 42% reduction from doubling one layer's thickness.

quiz.q:
A second critical-radius check

Problem. A different cable insulation has \(k_{ins}=0.20\ \text{W/mK}\), installed where the surrounding air convection coefficient is \(h=12\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\). Find the critical radius, and state what it means for a bare conductor of radius 12 mm.
quiz.solution: Solution. \(r_{cr}=k_{ins}/h=0.2/12.0=16.7\ \text{mm}\). Since the bare radius (12 mm) already exceeds \(r_{cr}\) (16.7 mm), this conductor is past the extremum — any insulation added immediately reduces heat loss; there is no backfire zone left to cross.

quiz.q: Which of the three rate laws in §1 is the only one that can transport heat with no material medium present at all?
choices: Conduction
Convection
Radiation
All three require a medium
quiz.solution: Radiation is carried by electromagnetic waves and crosses a vacuum; conduction and convection both require a material medium.

quiz.q: Fourier's law, Newton's law of cooling, and the Stefan–Boltzmann law all rearrange into which common structural form?
choices: A driving temperature difference divided by a thermal resistance
A pressure difference divided by a flow resistance
A mass flow rate times a specific heat
An entropy generation term times an ambient temperature
quiz.solution: Every rate law in §2 becomes \(q=\Delta T/R\) once rearranged — the thermal-circuit analogy this whole course leans on.

quiz.q: Why is a cylindrical shell's conduction resistance written as \(\ln(r_2/r_1)/(2\pi k L)\) rather than a plane-wall-style thickness over area?
choices: Because cylindrical conductivity is direction-dependent
Because the area available for conduction grows with radius, so a fixed radial increment does not carry a fixed resistance increment
Because heat cannot conduct radially, only axially
Because pipes are always thinner than plane walls
quiz.solution: Area grows as \(2\pi r L\), so the resistance contributed by each additional radial increment shrinks as r grows — hence the logarithmic form.

quiz.q: Doubling the convection coefficient \(h\) at a surface, area held fixed, does what to \(R_{conv}=1/(hA)\)?
choices: Doubles it
Halves it
Leaves it unchanged
Squares it
quiz.solution: \(R_{conv}\) is inversely proportional to \(h\); doubling \(h\) halves the resistance.

quiz.q: A windier installation site (higher external \(h\)) does what to the critical radius \(r_{cr}=k_{ins}/h\)?
choices: Increases it, widening the insulation-backfire zone
Decreases it, shrinking the insulation-backfire zone
Leaves it unchanged — \(r_{cr}\) depends only on the insulation
Makes the critical-radius effect disappear entirely regardless of bare radius
quiz.solution: \(r_{cr}\) is inversely proportional to \(h\); a higher \(h\) shrinks \(r_{cr}\) and with it the range of bare radii vulnerable to the effect.

quiz.q: A bare conductor's radius is already larger than \(r_{cr}\). What happens when insulation is added?
choices: Heat loss rises before it falls, exactly as for a thinner conductor
Heat loss falls immediately — this conductor is already past the extremum
Heat loss is unaffected by any amount of insulation
The critical-radius concept does not apply to conductors, only to plane walls
quiz.solution: The backfire zone only exists for bare radii below \(r_{cr}\); above it, \(R_{tot}\) is already increasing with added radius, so insulation helps immediately.

quiz.q: In §4's worked wall, roughly what share of the total resistance does the insulation layer alone carry?
choices: About 25%
About 50%
About 74%
Exactly 100% — the films and brick contribute nothing
quiz.solution: \(R_3/R_{tot}=1.250/1.693=74\%\) — by far the largest single share, which is why insulation is the first place a real audit looks.

quiz.q: A new layer is added in series to an existing resistance network, with perfect contact and no new convection surface created. What happens to \(R_{tot}\) and \(q\)?
choices: \(R_{tot}\) increases, \(q\) decreases
\(R_{tot}\) decreases, \(q\) increases
Neither changes
\(R_{tot}\) increases, \(q\) also increases
quiz.solution: Series resistances simply add; one more strictly-positive term always raises \(R_{tot}\), and for a fixed overall \(\Delta T\) that always lowers \(q=\Delta T/R_{tot}\).

1.kuwait:
A facilities engineer auditing a building envelope's heat gain checks which single layer dominates the resistance chain before recommending any upgrade — per §4's own numbers, a well-built wall's insulation layer alone can carry roughly three-quarters of the total resistance, so an audit that finds thin or missing insulation has found the highest-leverage fix, while re-specifying the indoor air film or the brick thickness would be a low-return distraction.

A technician insulating a small-diameter instrument-tracing line or control cable checks the critical radius before assuming that any added wrap helps — per §3, a modest layer on a thin enough conductor can raise, not lower, its heat loss until the wrap passes \(r_{cr}\), a genuinely counter-intuitive result worth a two-line check before insulating small-diameter lines as a matter of routine practice.

2.lecture:
§1 · Why extend a surface at all

Newton's law of cooling, \(q=hA(T_s-T_\infty)\), leaves exactly three levers once a surface and its fluid are fixed: the convection coefficient \(h\), the temperature difference, and the area \(A\). When \(h\) is the bottleneck — the gas side of an air-cooled enclosure, an un-fanned motor housing, any application where \(h\) sits in the tens of W/m²K rather than the hundreds or thousands a liquid delivers — and the temperature difference is already set by the process, the only lever left is area. A fin is exactly that: manufactured extra area, bought in exchange for material, cost, and — crucially, per §3 — a fin's own internal conduction resistance that eventually limits how much that extra area is actually worth.

§2 · The fin equation, derived and solved

An energy balance on a thin differential slice of a straight fin — conduction in one face, conduction out the other, the difference lost sideways by convection — gives, after dividing through by the slice's cross-sectional conduction term, the fin equation \(\dfrac{d^2\theta}{dx^2}-m^2\theta=0\), where \(\theta=T-T_\infty\) is the local temperature excess and \(m=\sqrt{hP/(kA_c)}\) bundles the perimeter \(P\) (convecting surface per unit length), cross-sectional area \(A_c\) (conducting area), conductivity \(k\), and \(h\) into one rate constant. With an adiabatic-tip idealization (the tip's own end-face loss neglected — reasonable once a fin is slender and not too short), the solution is \(\theta/\theta_b=\cosh(m(L-x))/\cosh(mL)\), and the total heat the fin carries away from its base works out to \(q_{fin}=\sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\tanh(mL)\).

§3 · Efficiency, effectiveness, and where fins stop paying

Fin efficiency \(\eta_{fin}=q_{fin}/q_{max}=\tanh(mL)/mL\) compares the real fin to a hypothetical one held entirely at base temperature — it falls as \(mL\) grows, because a longer or lower-conductivity fin loses more of its temperature excess before the tip. Fin effectiveness \(\varepsilon_{fin}=q_{fin}/(hA_c\theta_b)\) instead compares the fin to the same base area with no fin at all — a fin is worth adding once \(\varepsilon_{fin}\) climbs well past 1 (a common rule of thumb wants it past 2), and since \(\varepsilon_{fin}\) scales with \(\sqrt{k/h}\), fins pay off far more decisively on the low-\(h\) gas side of a system than on a high-\(h\) liquid or boiling side — the same \(k/h\) logic behind why air-cooled equipment bristles with fins and liquid-cooled equipment mostly does not. Length has diminishing returns for a separate reason: \(\tanh(mL)\) saturates toward 1 once \(mL\gtrsim 2.5\text{–}3\), so past that point almost no additional heat leaves per unit of added length — doubling this lesson's own 200 mm fin to 400 mm (§4) buys only 9% more heat rate for twice the material, which is where a fin design stops paying for itself.

§4 · A worked pin fin

An aluminum pin fin, \(k=200\ \text{W/mK}\), diameter 5 mm, length 200 mm, sits in \(h=15\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) air with a base excess \(\theta_b=60\)°C. \(A_c=\pi r^2=19.63\ \text{mm}^2\), \(P=\pi D=15.71\ \text{mm}\), \(m=\sqrt{hP/(kA_c)}=7.75\ \text{m}^{-1}\), \(mL=1.549\), \(\tanh(mL)=0.914\). Efficiency \(\eta_{fin}=\tanh(mL)/mL=59\%\); the fin delivers \(q_{fin}=\sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\tanh(mL)=1.67\ \text{W}\), and against its own bare cross-section, \(\varepsilon_{fin}=q_{fin}/(hA_c\theta_b)=94\) — comfortably past the worth-adding threshold. Doubling the length to 400 mm drops efficiency to 32% and raises \(q_{fin}\) only to 1.82 W, the 9% gain §3 already flagged as the point of diminishing returns.

Key relation — adiabatic-tip fin

\[ \eta_{fin}=\frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}, \qquad m=\sqrt{\frac{hP}{kA_c}} \]

Every fin-efficiency chart in every textbook is this same ratio, tabulated for other tip conditions and cross-sections.

quiz.q:
A worked pin fin

Problem. An aluminum pin fin (\(k=200\ \text{W/mK}\)), diameter 5 mm, length 200 mm, sits in \(h=15\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) air with base excess \(\theta_b=60\)°C. Find \(m\), \(mL\), the efficiency, and the heat rate \(q_{fin}\).

Doubling the fin length

Problem. The same fin is remade at 400 mm instead of 200 mm, all else unchanged. Find the new efficiency and heat rate, and the percent gain over the original 200 mm fin.
quiz.solution: Solution. \(mL=3.098\), \(\tanh(mL)=0.996\), \(\eta_{fin}=32\%\). \(q_{fin}=1.82\ \text{W}\), only \((1.82/1.67-1)\times100=9\%\) more than the 200 mm fin — twice the material for 9% more heat.

quiz.q:
The same fin, moved to a liquid

Problem. The original 200 mm fin (§4) is instead installed where \(h=200\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) (a liquid rather than air). Find the new \(q_{fin}\) and effectiveness \(\varepsilon_{fin}\), and compare \(\varepsilon_{fin}\) to the original air-side value.
quiz.solution: Solution. \(m=\sqrt{h_{liq}P/(kA_c)}=28.28\ \text{m}^{-1}\), \(mL=5.657\). \(q_{fin}=\sqrt{h_{liq}PkA_c}\,\theta_b\tanh(mL)=6.66\ \text{W}\) — larger in absolute terms than the air-side 1.67 W, because \(h\) itself is larger. But \(\varepsilon_{fin}=q_{fin}/(h_{liq}A_c\theta_b)=28\), down from 94 on the air side — a 3.3× drop in how much this fin is worth relative to the bare area it replaces.

quiz.q: A fin is most worth adding when which quantity is the bottleneck in \(q=hA(T_s-T_\infty)\)?
choices: The convection coefficient h, when it is low
The temperature difference, when it is large
The fin's own thermal conductivity, when it is low
The ambient pressure
quiz.solution: When h is already large (liquid, boiling), a fin's own internal conduction resistance limits its payoff; low h is where extra area helps most.

quiz.q: As the fin parameter \(mL\to 0\) (a very short or very conductive fin), what does the efficiency \(\eta_{fin}=\tanh(mL)/mL\) approach?
choices: It diverges to infinity
quiz.solution: \(\lim_{x\to0}\tanh(x)/x=1\): a short, highly conductive fin stays almost entirely at base temperature, so it performs almost as well as the all-base-temperature ideal.

quiz.q: This lesson's fin has \(m=7.75\ \text{m}^{-1}\). A different fin with a LARGER \(m\) (thinner, lower-k, or higher-h) decays in temperature excess along its length:
choices: More slowly than this fin
More quickly — the excess drops off over a shorter physical distance
At exactly the same rate — m does not affect the profile's shape
Only near the tip, never near the base
quiz.solution: \(\theta/\theta_b=\cosh(m(L-x))/\cosh(mL)\): a larger m compresses the same decay into a shorter distance — thinner or lower-conductivity fins 'use up' their base excess faster.

quiz.q: The adiabatic-tip idealization used in §2 assumes which loss is negligible?
choices: Convection loss along the fin's lateral (side) surface
Conduction loss from the fin's tip end-face
Radiation loss from the base
All losses — the fin is assumed perfectly insulated everywhere
quiz.solution: The lateral surface is exactly where the fin is supposed to lose heat; the idealization only neglects the small end-face area at the very tip.

quiz.q: Fin effectiveness \(\varepsilon_{fin}\) scales with which ratio, explaining why fins matter more on the gas side of a system than the liquid side?
choices: \(\sqrt{k/h}\)
\(k\cdot h\)
It does not depend on either k or h
quiz.solution: \(\varepsilon_{fin}\propto\sqrt{k/h}\): high-conductivity metal fins pay off most where h is small, i.e. gas-side, low-h convection.

quiz.q: In §4, doubling this lesson's fin from 200 mm to 400 mm changed the heat rate by roughly:
choices: +9% — barely more, despite double the material
+100% — heat rate doubled along with the length
-50% — heat rate fell
No change at all
quiz.solution: \(q_{fin}\) rose from 1.67 W to only 1.82 W — a 9% gain for double the material, exactly the saturation §3 describes.

quiz.q: Moving the same pin fin from air (h=15 W/m²K) to a liquid (h=200 W/m²K) does what to its absolute heat rate \(q_{fin}\) and its effectiveness \(\varepsilon_{fin}\)?
choices: Both rise
Both fall
\(q_{fin}\) rises (h itself is larger), but \(\varepsilon_{fin}\) falls (the fin is worth relatively less against its own higher-h bare area)
\(q_{fin}\) falls, but \(\varepsilon_{fin}\) rises
quiz.solution: Absolute heat rate tracks h directly and rises; but effectiveness compares the fin to its own bare area at that same higher h, and that comparison gets tougher as h rises — both verified in this lesson's third solve problem.

quiz.q: A designer is choosing where to add fins across four surfaces on the same machine. Which surface benefits LEAST?
choices: A sealed, un-fanned electrical enclosure wall in still air
The gas side of an air-cooled cylinder head
A tube surface undergoing nucleate boiling on the process side
An outdoor motor housing cooled by natural convection only
quiz.solution: Boiling delivers a very high h; per §3's \(\sqrt{k/h}\) scaling, fins pay off least where h is already high — exactly the regime Lesson 7 covers next.

2.kuwait:
A design engineer specifying an air-cooled heat sink or a finned outdoor enclosure checks fin effectiveness, not just fin count, before adding material — per §3, effectiveness scales with \(\sqrt{k/h}\), which is exactly why finning pays off decisively on gas-cooled, low-\(h\) outdoor equipment and offers far less on a liquid-cooled jacket, where the same aluminum and the same geometry would buy proportionally less.

An engineer reviewing a finned air-cooled housing design recognizes, per §3's own doubling-length result, that past a certain length additional fin material buys almost nothing — a design that keeps extending fin length chasing a marginal cooling gain is spending mass and cost for a return close to noise, an easy, quantifiable objection to raise in a design review rather than a vague one.

3.lecture:
§1 · Lumped capacitance, and its entry ticket

If a body's own internal conduction is fast enough that it stays at one uniform temperature while it heats or cools, an energy balance on the whole body — stored energy lost to convection, \(\rho V c\,dT/dt=-hA(T-T_\infty)\) — integrates directly to \(\dfrac{T-T_\infty}{T_i-T_\infty}=e^{-t/\tau}\), a single exponential set by one time constant \(\tau=\rho V c/(hA)\). The entry ticket for this shortcut is the Biot number, \(Bi=hL_c/k\) with \(L_c=V/A\): it is a ratio of internal conduction resistance \((L_c/k)\) to external convection resistance \((1/h)\), and the common rule of thumb is \(Bi<0.1\) — internal resistance small enough next to the external bottleneck that "uniform temperature" is a good approximation, not merely a convenient one.

§2 · When lumped fails: the semi-infinite solid and one-term charts

When \(Bi\) is not small — a thick section, a highly conductive-but-large body, or simply the first instants after a sudden surface change, before the interior has had time to feel it — a body is better modeled as a semi-infinite solid: an error-function temperature profile spreading in from the surface, whose surface heat flux falls as \(1/\sqrt{t}\), because the depth the disturbance has reached keeps growing while the local gradient keeps relaxing. For finite shapes (a plane wall, a long cylinder, a sphere) once enough time has passed that a single mode dominates (\(Fo=\alpha t/L_c^2\gtrsim0.2\)), a one-term solution \(\theta^*=C_1 e^{-\lambda_1^2 Fo}\) (times a position function), with \(\lambda_1\) and \(C_1\) tabulated against \(Bi\) and geometry, replaces the full infinite series — the same role Heisler charts play by hand.

§3 · The same V/A ratio, a different exponent

Both \(\tau=\rho V c/(hA)\) here and Chvorinov's rule for casting solidification time (\(t\propto(V/A)^2\), covered in this curriculum's manufacturing-processes course) are governed by the same geometric ratio, \(V/A\) — a body's volume against the surface it must lose or gain heat through. The exponent differs because the limiting resistance differs: lumped capacitance's \(\tau\) is externally convection-limited, and \(V/A\) enters linearly through the energy balance; Chvorinov's rule describes a growing solid shell whose own conduction resistance is what's controlling, and that internally-limited problem is what pushes the exponent to two. Estimating a real time honestly means checking \(Bi\) first and picking the model that ticket actually admits — never assuming lumped capacitance applies just because it is the easier formula to reach for.

§4 · A worked quench, checked before it is trusted

A 12 mm-diameter steel pin (\(k=45\ \text{W/mK}\), \(\rho=7850\ \text{kg/m}^3\), \(c=470\ \text{J/kgK}\)) is quenched in agitated oil, \(h=750\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\). For a long cylinder, \(L_c=V/A=r/2=3.0\ \ \text{mm}\), so \(Bi=hL_c/k=0.05\) — comfortably under 0.1, lumped capacitance is legitimate. \(\tau=\rho L_c c/h=14.76\ \text{s}\): physically, the time for the excess temperature to fall to \(1/e\approx37\%\) of its start. Reaching 5% of the initial excess takes \(t=\tau\ln(20)=44.2\ \text{s}\); reaching 10% takes \(t=\tau\ln(10)=34.0\ \ \text{s}\). Had the same pin instead been quenched in water (\(h=5000\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\)), \(Bi\) would climb to 0.33 — over the 0.1 line, meaning the lumped shortcut is no longer legitimate for that case, exactly the honest check §3 insists on before trusting any of these numbers.

Key relation — lumped capacitance

\[ \frac{T-T_\infty}{T_i-T_\infty}=e^{-t/\tau}, \qquad \tau=\frac{\rho V c}{hA}, \qquad Bi=\frac{hL_c}{k}<0.1 \]

Check \(Bi\) before trusting \(\tau\) — the formula does not warn you when it stops applying.

quiz.q:
The quenched pin, checked and timed

Problem. A 12 mm-diameter steel pin (\(k=45\ \text{W/mK}\), \(\rho=7850\ \text{kg/m}^3\), \(c=470\ \text{J/kgK}\)) is quenched in oil at \(h=750\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\). Check the Biot number, then find \(\tau\) and the time to reach 5% of the initial excess.
quiz.solution: Solution. \(L_c=r/2=3.0\ \text{mm}\), \(Bi=hL_c/k=0.05<0.1\): lumped is valid. \(\tau=\rho L_c c/h=14.76\ \text{s}\). \(t_{5\%}=\tau\ln(20)=44.2\ \text{s}\).

quiz.q:
The same pin, quenched in water instead

Problem. The identical pin is quenched in water, \(h=5000\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\), instead of oil. Check whether lumped capacitance is still legitimate.
quiz.solution: Solution. \(Bi=h L_c/k=5000\times0.0030/45=0.33\) — over the 0.1 threshold. Lumped capacitance is no longer a legitimate model for this case; a one-term or chart-based solution (§2) is required instead.

quiz.q:
Doubling the pin's radius

Problem. A second pin, identical material and quench (\(h=750\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\)), has twice the diameter (24 mm instead of 12 mm). Find its Biot number and time constant, and compare \(\tau\) to the original pin's.
quiz.solution: Solution. \(L_c=r/2=6\ \text{mm}\), \(Bi=hL_c/k=0.10\) — right at the 0.1 boundary, lumped capacitance is only just still defensible. \(\tau=\rho L_c c/h=29.52\ \text{s}\), exactly \(2\times\) the original 14.76 s — \(\tau\) scales linearly with \(L_c=V/A\), not with its square as Chvorinov's rule does.

quiz.q: The Biot number \(Bi=hL_c/k\) is best understood as a ratio of which two quantities?
choices: External convection resistance to internal conduction resistance
Internal conduction resistance to external convection resistance
Stored thermal energy to heat lost per second
Surface area to volume, with no resistance interpretation
quiz.solution: \(Bi=(L_c/k)/(1/h)\) — internal conduction resistance in the numerator, external convection resistance in the denominator.

quiz.q: A body's Biot number is 0.6. Which model should be used for its transient response?
choices: Lumped capacitance — Bi is small enough
A one-term (chart-based) or full-series solution — Bi is too large for lumped capacitance
Neither model applies to any body with Bi above zero
The semi-infinite solid solution, forever, regardless of elapsed time
quiz.solution: \(Bi=0.6\) is well above the \(0.1\) rule-of-thumb line; internal conduction resistance is not negligible, so lumped capacitance is not a legitimate shortcut here.

quiz.q: Near the surface of a suddenly-heated semi-infinite solid, the surface heat flux behaves as time proceeds:
choices: It stays constant forever
It rises without bound
It falls, proportional to \(1/\sqrt{t}\)
It falls to exactly zero after one time constant
quiz.solution: As the affected depth grows, the local temperature gradient at the surface relaxes, and the semi-infinite solid solution gives a surface flux falling as \(1/\sqrt{t}\).

quiz.q: Both this lesson's lumped time constant and casting's Chvorinov's rule are governed by the same geometric ratio. Which one?
choices: Surface area alone
Volume alone
\(V/A\), the volume-to-surface-area ratio
Density times specific heat
quiz.solution: Both \(\tau=\rho Vc/(hA)\) and Chvorinov's \(t\propto(V/A)^2\) are built on \(V/A\) — but with different exponents, per §3.

quiz.q: Why does Chvorinov's rule use \((V/A)^2\) while this lesson's lumped \(\tau\) uses \(V/A\) to the first power?
choices: It is an arbitrary historical convention with no physical cause
Because Chvorinov's problem is limited by the growing solid shell's own internal conduction resistance, not by external convection
Because casting always involves larger volumes than quenching
Because Chvorinov's rule is only an approximation and the exponent is not meaningful
quiz.solution: Lumped capacitance is externally (convection) limited, linear in V/A; Chvorinov's solidifying shell is internally (conduction) limited, which is what pushes the exponent to two — a real physical distinction, not a coincidence.

quiz.q: In this lesson's worked pin, what does reaching \(t=\tau\) specifically correspond to?
choices: The temperature excess has fallen to zero
The temperature excess has fallen to \(1/e\approx37\%\) of its initial value
The part has reached exactly the ambient temperature
The Biot number has changed
quiz.solution: By definition of the exponential solution, one time constant is the time for the excess to fall to \(1/e\) of its starting value — visible directly on this lesson's diagram.

quiz.q: Doubling a lumped body's characteristic length \(L_c\) (same material, same h) changes \(Bi\) and \(\tau\) how?
choices: Both double
Bi halves, τ doubles
Both are unchanged
Bi doubles, τ quadruples
quiz.solution: Both \(Bi=hL_c/k\) and \(\tau=\rho L_c c/h\) are directly proportional to \(L_c\); doubling \(L_c\) doubles both, exactly as this lesson's third solve problem shows numerically.

quiz.q: A one-term (Heisler-chart-style) solution is appropriate once which condition is met?
choices: Immediately at t=0, before anything else is checked
Once the Fourier number \(Fo=\alpha t/L_c^2\) is roughly 0.2 or larger
Only when Bi is exactly zero
Never — the full infinite series is always required
quiz.solution: Once \(Fo\gtrsim0.2\), the series solution is dominated by its first term, and the one-term/chart approximation becomes accurate enough for engineering use.

3.kuwait:
A technician estimating quench or preheat duration for a batch of small parts checks the Biot number before reaching for the lumped-capacitance formula at all — per §1 and §4, the same part can sit comfortably under the \(Bi<0.1\) line in one quench medium and clear it entirely in a more aggressive one, and applying the easy formula regardless of which medium is in use would misestimate the time by a real, checkable margin.

An engineer estimating how long a hot component in a shut-down process line takes to reach a safe-to-touch temperature uses the same time-constant idea, once \(Bi\) is confirmed small, as a first-pass lockout-duration estimate — a legitimate, quick engineering number precisely because the check in §1 was not skipped first.

4.lecture:
§1 · Two boundary layers, and the laminar–turbulent split

A fluid moving past a solid surface develops a velocity boundary layer: zero velocity at the wall (no-slip), rising to the free-stream value across a thin layer of thickness \(\delta\). If the surface and fluid are at different temperatures, a parallel thermal boundary layer develops the same way, wall temperature to free-stream temperature across thickness \(\delta_t\). Close to a leading edge this layer is smooth and layered — laminar; far enough along, it becomes chaotic and well-mixed except for a thin viscous sublayer — turbulent. For a flat plate the transition sits near \(Re_{x,crit}\approx5\times10^5\), a Reynolds number, not a fixed distance — it depends on velocity and fluid as much as on position.

§2 · The Nu–Re–Pr grammar

The Nusselt number \(Nu=hL/k\) is simply a dimensionless convection coefficient — how much better convection moves heat across length scale \(L\) than pure conduction through a stationary layer of the same fluid would. Forced-convection correlations fit \(Nu\) as a power law in the Reynolds number \(Re=VL/\nu\) (inertial versus viscous forces) and the Prandtl number \(Pr=\nu/\alpha\) (momentum versus thermal diffusivity — physically, how the velocity and thermal boundary layers compare in thickness): \(Nu=C\,Re^m Pr^n\). For laminar flow averaged over a flat plate, \(Nu_L=0.664\,Re_L^{0.5}Pr^{1/3}\) (valid for \(Pr\gtrsim0.6\)). Cylinders in crossflow and tube banks follow the same structural form with different constants and, for banks, a row-count correction — always fitted to data over a stated range, never derived from first principles the way the flat-plate boundary-layer result nearly is.

§3 · Using a correlation without believing it past its range

Every correlation in §2 carries fine print: a Reynolds range, a Prandtl range, a specific geometry it was fitted to. Applying a flat-plate correlation to a cylinder in crossflow is not a rounding error — it is a category error, since the boundary-layer physics genuinely differs (a cylinder's boundary layer separates; a flat plate's does not). Applying a correlation validated for \(Pr\gtrsim0.6\) to a very low-\(Pr\) liquid metal is the same mistake in a quieter disguise: the fitted exponent on \(Pr\) was never tested down there, and there is no guarantee it still holds.

§4 · A worked flat plate

Air at \(V=5\ \text{m/s}\) flows over a 200 mm-long flat plate (an electronics board or a heat-sink base), film properties \(\nu=1.6e-05\ \ \text{m}^2\text{/s}\), \(k=0.027\ \text{W/mK}\), \(Pr=0.71\). \(Re_L=VL/\nu=62,500\) — well under \(Re_{crit}\), laminar the whole length. \(Nu_L=0.664\,Re_L^{0.5}Pr^{1/3}=148.1\), giving \(h=Nu_L k/L=19.99\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) — a modest number, typical of low-speed forced air, and the reason Lesson 2's fins matter so much on exactly this kind of surface.

Key relation — laminar flat plate

\[ Nu_L=\frac{hL}{k}=0.664\,Re_L^{0.5}Pr^{1/3} \]

Same grammar, different constants, for every other forced-convection correlation this course uses.
4.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Newton's law of cooling (Lesson 1) treated \(h\) as empirical and externally given; this lesson is where \(h\) finally gets computed from first principles rather than assumed.

quiz.q:
The worked flat plate

Problem. Air at \(V=5\ \text{m/s}\) flows over a 200 mm flat plate, \(\nu=1.6e-05\ \text{m}^2\text{/s}\), \(k=0.027\ \text{W/mK}\), \(Pr=0.71\). Find \(Re_L\), confirm the flow stays laminar, then find \(Nu_L\) and \(h\).
quiz.solution: Solution. \(Re_L=VL/\nu=62,500\), under \(Re_{crit}=5\times10^5\): laminar throughout. \(Nu_L=0.664\,Re_L^{0.5}Pr^{1/3}=148.1\). \(h=Nu_L k/L=19.99\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\).

quiz.q:
Doubling the approach velocity

Problem. The same plate now sees \(V=10\ \text{m/s}\), all else unchanged. Find the new \(Re_L\), \(Nu_L\), and \(h\), and the ratio to the original h.
quiz.solution: Solution. \(Re_L=125,000\) (still laminar). \(Nu_L=0.664\,Re_L^{0.5}Pr^{1/3}=209.4\). \(h=28.27\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\). Ratio \(h_{new}/h_{old}=1.414\), matching \(\sqrt{2}=1.414\) exactly, since \(h\propto Re^{0.5}\propto V^{0.5}\) at fixed L.

quiz.q:
Checking the correlation's own range

Problem. The same plate, same \(V=5\ \text{m/s}\) and fluid, is now 2 m long instead of 200 mm. Check whether the laminar flat-plate correlation used in §4 still applies over the full length.
quiz.solution: Solution. \(Re_L=VL/\nu=625,000\), which exceeds \(Re_{crit}=5\times10^5\). The boundary layer transitions to turbulent before the trailing edge, so the purely laminar correlation no longer applies over the full 2 m — a mixed laminar-turbulent correlation would be required instead, exactly the range check §3 insists on.

quiz.q: Physically, what does the Nusselt number \(Nu=hL/k\) represent?
choices: The ratio of inertial to viscous forces in the flow
How much better convection moves heat across length L than pure conduction through a stationary layer of the same fluid
The ratio of momentum to thermal diffusivity
The fraction of the boundary layer that is turbulent
quiz.solution: Nu compares the actual convective h to the conduction that would occur across the same length scale L through the still fluid — a dimensionless convection coefficient.

quiz.q: For a flat plate, the laminar-to-turbulent boundary-layer transition is associated with a Reynolds number near:
quiz.solution: The classic flat-plate transition Reynolds number is roughly \(5\times10^5\), used directly in this lesson's own worked example.

quiz.q: In \(Nu_L=0.664\,Re_L^{0.5}Pr^{1/3}\), doubling the free-stream velocity V (plate length and fluid fixed) multiplies h by approximately:
quiz.solution: \(h\propto Re^{0.5}\propto V^{0.5}\); doubling V multiplies h by \(\sqrt{2}\approx1.41\), confirmed numerically in this lesson's second solve problem.

quiz.q: Applying a flat-plate forced-convection correlation to a cylinder in crossflow is best described as:
choices: A safe approximation, since both are just 'external convection'
A category error — a cylinder's boundary layer separates, which a flat plate's does not, so the underlying physics genuinely differs
Exact, provided the same Reynolds number is used
Only wrong if the fluid is a liquid metal
quiz.solution: Flow separation around a cylinder is physics a flat-plate correlation was never fitted to; §3 calls this a category error, not a rounding error.

quiz.q: A correlation is validated for \(Pr\gtrsim0.6\). Applying it to a liquid metal (\(Pr\ll1\)) risks:
choices: No risk at all — Pr never actually affects the result
A large, unquantified error, since the fitted Pr exponent was never tested in that range
Only a small, easily-corrected rounding error
Automatic conversion of the correlation to a natural-convection form
quiz.solution: A correlation's exponents are empirical fits over a stated range; extrapolating far outside it — as with liquid metals at very low Pr — has no guarantee of holding.

quiz.q: On a laminar flat plate, the boundary-layer thickness \(\delta(x)\) grows approximately as:
choices: Linearly with x
As \(\sqrt{x}\)
It stays constant
quiz.solution: The classic laminar result \(\delta/x=5/\sqrt{Re_x}\) gives \(\delta\propto\sqrt{x}\), the shape plotted in this lesson's own diagram.

quiz.q: In this lesson's worked example (\(Re_L=62,500\)), the 200 mm plate is:
choices: Entirely turbulent from the leading edge
Entirely laminar — \(Re_L\) never approaches \(Re_{crit}\approx5\times10^5\)
Laminar for the first half, turbulent for the second
Impossible to classify without the plate's material
quiz.solution: \(Re_L=62,500\) is well under \(5\times10^5\); the entire plate length stays laminar, consistent with the smooth, unbroken growth curve in this lesson's diagram.

quiz.q: The Prandtl number \(Pr=\nu/\alpha\) physically compares which two quantities?
choices: Inertial forces to viscous forces
Momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity — roughly, how the velocity and thermal boundary layers compare in thickness
Convective to conductive heat transfer
Buoyancy forces to viscous forces
quiz.solution: Pr is the ratio of momentum diffusivity (ν) to thermal diffusivity (α); a high Pr fluid has a thermal boundary layer thinner than its velocity boundary layer, and vice versa.

4.kuwait:
An engineer sizing air-cooled equipment checks a correlation's stated Reynolds and Prandtl range before trusting its predicted h — per §3, borrowing a flat-plate result for a cylinder, or a correlation's fitted Prandtl exponent for a fluid well outside the range it was tested on, is a category error a spec sheet's fine print would have caught before it ever reached a design calculation.

A technician noticing an air-cooled component running hotter than usual in summer checks whether actual airflow — not just ambient temperature — has quietly dropped, since a fouled filter or a degrading fan reduces \(V\) directly; per §4's own correlation, \(h\propto V^{0.5}\), so even a partial airflow loss produces a real, calculable derating in cooling capacity, not merely a vague seasonal excuse.

5.lecture:
§1 · The entrance region, and what "fully developed" really means

Flow entering a pipe with a uniform inlet profile takes a hydrodynamic entry length to settle into its fully developed velocity shape, and — separately — a thermal entry length to settle into a fully developed temperature profile. "Thermally fully developed" is a specific, easy-to-misread claim: it means the dimensionless temperature profile shape stops changing with distance, not that the fluid's actual bulk temperature stops changing — the bulk temperature keeps rising (or falling) the entire pipe length; only the shape of the profile riding on top of it has settled down.

§2 · Constant flux versus constant wall temperature

For laminar, fully developed flow, the two common thermal boundary conditions give genuinely different Nusselt numbers even at identical Reynolds and Prandtl numbers: \(Nu=4.36\) for constant surface heat flux, \(Nu=3.66\) for constant surface temperature — different because the boundary condition itself reshapes the developed temperature profile. For turbulent flow, the Dittus–Boelter correlation \(Nu=0.023\,Re^{0.8}Pr^{n}\) (\(n=0.4\) heating the fluid, \(n=0.3\) cooling it) is the workhorse, with its own fine print: \(Re\gtrsim10{,}000\), \(0.6\lesssim Pr\lesssim160\), and \(L/D\gtrsim10\) — short pipes still carry an enhanced entrance-region \(h\) this fully developed correlation does not capture, so applying it uncorrected to a short pipe underpredicts the true average \(h\), not overpredicts it.

§3 · Heat pickup along a real pipe run

An energy balance on the fluid, \(\dot m c_p\,dT_m/dx=q''P\), integrates very differently depending on which quantity is held constant. Constant heat flux gives a bulk temperature that rises linearly with \(x\) — a fixed \(q''\) delivers a fixed temperature rise per metre, the whole length. Constant wall temperature instead gives \(\ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_s-T_o}{T_s-T_i}\right)=-\dfrac{hPL}{\dot m c_p}\), an exponential approach toward \(T_s\) that never quite arrives. A steam-jacketed pipe is the constant-\(T_s\) case: steam condensing on the jacket's exterior releases a large latent heat at essentially one saturation temperature, holding the wall close to a fixed value regardless of how much heat the process fluid inside is drawing — unlike, say, a uniformly electrically heated pipe, which is closer to the constant-flux case instead.

§4 · A worked steam-jacketed pipe

Water at \(\dot m=0.5\ \text{kg/s}\) (\(c_p=4180\ \text{J/kgK}\)) enters a \(D=50\ \text{mm}\), \(L=10\ \text{m}\) pipe at \(T_i=20\)°C; the jacket holds the wall at \(T_s=120\)°C, with \(h=1500\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\). \(P=\pi D=0.1571\ \text{m}\), so \(hPL/(\dot m c_p)=1.127\) and \((T_s-T_o)/(T_s-T_i)=e^{-1.127}=0.324\), giving \(T_o=T_s-0.324(T_s-T_i)=87.6\)°C — a substantial rise for a single 10 m run. As a check on the assumed \(h\): using representative water properties at the pipe's mean film temperature (\(\mu\approx5.5e-04\ \text{Pa·s}\), \(k\approx0.64\ \ \text{W/mK}\), \(Pr\approx3.5\) — given, representative values, not a claimed precise lookup), \(Re=4\dot m/(\pi D\mu)=23,150\) (turbulent), and Dittus–Boelter gives \(Nu=0.023\,Re^{0.8}Pr^{0.4}=117.8\), \(h=1507\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) — within 1% of the 1500 W/m²K assumed, a reassuring cross-check rather than a coincidence.

Key relation — constant wall temperature

\[ \frac{T_s-T_o}{T_s-T_i}=\exp\!\left(-\frac{hPL}{\dot m c_p}\right) \]

An exponential approach, never a straight ramp — the signature of a constant-\(T_s\) boundary.

quiz.q:
The worked steam-jacketed pipe

Problem. Water, \(\dot m=0.5\ \text{kg/s}\), \(c_p=4180\ \text{J/kgK}\), enters a \(D=50\ \text{mm}\), \(L=10\ \text{m}\) pipe at \(T_i=20\)°C. The jacket holds \(T_s=120\)°C, \(h=1500\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\). Find the exit temperature.

Cross-checking h against Dittus–Boelter

Problem. For the same pipe and flow, representative water properties give \(\mu\approx5.5e-04\ \text{Pa·s}\), \(k\approx0.64\ \text{W/mK}\), \(Pr\approx3.5\). Find \(Re\), confirm turbulence, then find \(Nu\) and \(h\) from Dittus–Boelter, and compare to the assumed 1500 W/m²K.
quiz.solution: Solution. \(Re=4\dot m/(\pi D\mu)=23,150\) — turbulent, and \(L/D=200\) clears the \(\gtrsim10\) entrance-length rule. \(Nu=0.023\,Re^{0.8}Pr^{0.4}=117.8\), \(h=Nu\,k/D=1507\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) — within about 1% of the 1500 W/m²K assumed.

quiz.q:
Doubling the pipe length

Problem. The same jacketed pipe is extended to \(L=20\ \text{m}\), all else unchanged. Find the new exit temperature, and the additional temperature rise bought by the second 10 m compared with the first.
quiz.solution: Solution. \(hPL/(\dot m c_p)=2.255\), \((T_s-T_o)/(T_s-T_i)=e^{-2.255}=0.105\), \(T_o=120-0.105\times(120-20)=109.5\)°C. The first 10 m bought \(87.6-20=67.6\)°C of rise; the second 10 m bought only \(109.5-87.6=21.9\)°C more — the exponential approach paying off far less on the back half of the pipe.

quiz.q: For internal pipe flow, "thermally fully developed" specifically means:
choices: The bulk fluid temperature stops changing with x
The dimensionless temperature profile shape stops changing with x, even though the bulk temperature keeps changing
The flow has become turbulent
The wall temperature has become uniform
quiz.solution: The bulk temperature keeps evolving the whole pipe length; only the shape of the profile riding on it has settled down, per §1.

quiz.q: At identical Re and Pr, laminar fully developed pipe flow gives \(Nu=4.36\) for constant heat flux but \(Nu=3.66\) for constant wall temperature. Why do these differ?
choices: They do not actually differ; this is a textbook rounding artifact
The thermal boundary condition itself reshapes the developed temperature profile
Constant flux only applies to turbulent flow
Pr must be different in the two cases
quiz.solution: Different boundary conditions produce genuinely different developed temperature profile shapes, and Nu is a property of that profile.

quiz.q: Applying Dittus–Boelter, uncorrected, to a short pipe with \(L/D<10\) tends to:
choices: Overpredict the true average h, since entrance effects add extra resistance
Underpredict the true average h, since the entrance region actually carries an enhanced h that a fully developed correlation does not include
Have no effect — Dittus–Boelter is valid at any L/D
Only affect laminar flow, never turbulent flow
quiz.solution: The entrance region carries a higher true h than the fully developed value; a fully developed correlation applied to a short pipe misses that enhancement and underpredicts.

quiz.q: A steam jacket condensing on a pipe's exterior holds the wall close to constant temperature primarily because:
choices: Steam has an unusually high thermal conductivity
Condensation releases a large latent heat at essentially one saturation temperature, regardless of the process-side duty
The jacket is electrically heated at fixed power
Steam jackets are always thin enough to ignore
quiz.solution: Latent heat release at a fixed saturation temperature is what pins the wall near a constant value — unlike a fixed-power electric heater, which is closer to constant flux.

quiz.q: Under constant heat flux, the pipe's bulk mean temperature rises with distance x:
choices: Linearly
Exponentially, approaching an asymptote
Logarithmically
It falls, not rises
quiz.solution: \(\dot m c_p\,dT_m/dx=q''P=\)const integrates directly to a linear rise in \(T_m\) with x.

quiz.q: Under constant wall temperature, the bulk mean temperature approaches \(T_s\):
choices: Linearly, reaching \(T_s\) at a finite length
Exponentially — always approaching, never exactly arriving within a finite length
Instantaneously at the inlet
Only if the flow is laminar
quiz.solution: The governing equation integrates to the exponential-approach form derived in §3 and used throughout this lesson's worked example.

quiz.q: In this lesson's own numbers, doubling the pipe length from 10 m to 20 m added how much additional temperature rise, compared with the first 10 m?
choices: About the same again (roughly 68°C more)
Much less — only about 22°C more, versus 67.6°C from the first 10 m
Exactly zero — length beyond 10 m has no effect
More than the first stretch, not less
quiz.solution: The exponential approach means each additional metre buys less than the one before; the second 10 m added only 21.9°C versus the first stretch's 67.6°C, verified in this lesson's third solve problem.

quiz.q: This lesson's assumed \(h=1500\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) was cross-checked against which correlation?
choices: The laminar constant-flux result, Nu=4.36
Dittus–Boelter, using representative turbulent water properties
The flat-plate correlation from Lesson 4
It was not cross-checked at all
quiz.solution: §4's cross-check computes Re, confirms turbulence, and applies Dittus–Boelter, landing within about 1% of the assumed value.

5.kuwait:
An engineer sizing a jacketed or coil heat-traced pipe run identifies which thermal boundary condition actually applies before reaching for a formula — per §3, a condensing-steam jacket behaves as constant wall temperature, giving an exponential approach that delivers most of its temperature rise in the first few metres, while an electrically traced line closer to constant flux delivers its rise linearly instead; treating one as the other misestimates exactly where along the run the temperature change actually happens.

A maintenance engineer troubleshooting a short jacketed spool that underperforms its rated duty checks the pipe's \(L/D\) against Dittus–Boelter's own \(L/D\gtrsim10\) fine print before assuming the correlation used to size it was ever valid — per §2, a short run actually carries a higher true average h from its entrance region than the fully developed correlation predicts, so the correlation itself, not the hardware, may be the source of an apparent underperformance.

6.lecture:
§1 · Grashof and Rayleigh — natural convection's own dimensionless numbers

Natural convection has no imposed velocity to build a Reynolds number from — buoyancy itself drives the flow. The Grashof number, \(Gr=g\beta\Delta T L^3/\nu^2\), compares buoyancy force to viscous force, playing the role Reynolds number plays in forced convection. Multiplying by Prandtl number gives the Rayleigh number, \(Ra=Gr\cdot Pr\), which folds in a comparison of buoyancy to thermal diffusion as well — and it is \(Ra\), not \(Gr\) and \(Pr\) separately, that almost every natural-convection correlation is actually written in terms of.

§2 · Correlations, and why orientation changes everything

A vertical plate or enclosure wall follows a Churchill–Chu-type correlation, \(Nu=0.68+\dfrac{0.670\,Ra^{1/4}}{[1+(0.492/Pr)^{9/16}]^{4/9}}\) for the laminar range (\(Ra\lesssim10^9\)). Orientation matters far more here than in forced convection: a horizontal plate with its hot face up lets buoyant plumes rise away freely, giving a meaningfully higher \(h\); the same plate hot face down traps warm, buoyant fluid directly underneath a cooler layer above it — a stable arrangement with almost none of the free plume motion, and a correspondingly much lower \(h\). The same \(\Delta T\), the same plate, a different number, purely from which way it faces.

§3 · The Gulf-summer double penalty

A sealed, un-fanned electrical enclosure rejects heat by natural convection (and radiation, Lesson 9) alone. Two effects compound against it as ambient temperature climbs: \(h\) itself depends only weakly on \(\Delta T\) (roughly \(\Delta T^{1/4}\) in the laminar range, per §2's correlation), so it barely improves even as the surface runs hotter; meanwhile the available \(\Delta T\) between a fixed-rating surface and a hotter ambient shrinks directly. Both effects point the same unfavorable way at once — a genuine double penalty, not merely "it's hotter outside so everything runs a bit warmer."

§4 · A worked cabinet wall

A sealed cabinet's vertical wall, height \(L=0.3\ \text{m}\), runs at \(T_s=70\)°C in \(T_\infty=45\)°C still Gulf-summer air (\(\Delta T=25\)°C). At the film temperature, \(Pr=0.71\), \(Gr=5.85e+07\), \(Ra=Gr\cdot Pr=4.16e+07\) — under \(10^9\), the laminar correlation applies. \(Nu=42.0\), giving \(h=Nu\,k/L=3.99\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) — a strikingly low number next to Lesson 4's forced-air 20 W/m²K, and the reason natural convection sits at the very bottom of this lesson's own comparison chart. If ambient instead climbs to \(T_\infty=50\)°C (\(\Delta T=20\)°C) at the same surface temperature, \(h\) falls only slightly to 3.77 W/m²K, but the heat actually rejected, \(q''=h\Delta T\), falls from 99.7 to 75.4 W/m² — a 24% drop in cooling capacity from a mere 5°C rise in ambient, the double penalty of §3 made concrete.

Key relation — natural convection

\[ Ra=Gr\cdot Pr=\frac{g\beta\Delta T L^3}{\nu^2}\cdot Pr \]

Almost every correlation in this lesson is stated as \(Nu=f(Ra)\) — one number replaces the separate Reynolds and Prandtl numbers forced convection needed.

The worked cabinet wall

Problem. A cabinet wall, \(L=0.3\ \text{m}\) tall, runs at \(T_s=70\)°C in \(T_\infty=45\)°C air (\(Pr=0.71\), film properties as in §4). Find \(Gr\), \(Ra\), \(Nu\), and \(h\).
quiz.solution: Solution. \(Gr=g\beta\Delta TL^3/\nu^2=5.85e+07\). \(Ra=Gr\cdot Pr=4.16e+07\) (laminar range). \(Nu=42.0\). \(h=Nu\,k/L=3.99\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\).

quiz.q:
A hotter ambient, same surface temperature

Problem. The same wall stays at \(T_s=70\)°C, but ambient rises to \(T_\infty=50\)°C. Find the new h and the new heat flux \(q''=h\Delta T\), and compare the heat flux to the original 45°C-ambient case.
quiz.solution: Solution. \(\Delta T=20\)°C now. Recomputing, \(Ra=3.31e+07\), \(Nu=39.7\), \(h=3.77\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) — only slightly lower than 3.99. But \(q''=h\Delta T=75.4\ \text{W/m}^2\), down from 99.7 W/m² — a 24% drop in rejected heat from a 5°C ambient rise alone.

quiz.q:
A shorter cabinet wall

Problem. A second, shorter cabinet has a wall only \(L=0.15\ \text{m}\) tall, same \(T_s=70\)°C and \(T_\infty=45\)°C as §4. Find its Ra, Nu, and h, and compare h to the taller 0.3 m wall.
quiz.solution: Solution. \(Ra=5.20e+06\) (still laminar range), \(Nu=25.2\), \(h=4.80\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) — higher than the taller wall's 3.99 W/m²K, since \(h=Nu\,k/L\) and \(Nu\) grows more slowly than \(L\) does; natural-convection h weakly falls as the characteristic length grows, the opposite of what 'bigger surface, better cooling' might suggest.

quiz.q: The Grashof number \(Gr=g\beta\Delta TL^3/\nu^2\) plays which role in natural convection that Reynolds number plays in forced convection?
choices: It compares buoyancy force to viscous force, since there is no imposed velocity to build Re from
It compares momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity
It measures the fraction of flow that is turbulent
It replaces the Nusselt number entirely
quiz.solution: With no external velocity, buoyancy itself drives the flow, and Gr compares that buoyant driving force to viscous resistance — the natural-convection analog of Re.

quiz.q: The Rayleigh number \(Ra=Gr\cdot Pr\) adds which comparison beyond Gr alone?
choices: A comparison of buoyancy to thermal diffusion, not just viscosity
A comparison of inertial to viscous forces
A comparison of radiative to convective heat transfer
Nothing — Ra and Gr are interchangeable
quiz.solution: Multiplying by Pr (momentum vs. thermal diffusivity) folds thermal diffusion into the comparison, which is why almost every correlation is fitted to Ra directly.

quiz.q: A horizontal hot plate facing UP versus the same plate facing DOWN, same ΔT:
choices: Has identical h in both orientations
Has higher h facing up — free plumes rise away, versus down, where stable stratification suppresses motion
Has higher h facing down, since heat 'wants' to sink
Cannot be compared without knowing the fluid
quiz.solution: Facing up allows buoyant plumes to rise freely; facing down traps stable stratified fluid beneath a cooler layer, suppressing motion and lowering h substantially.

quiz.q: In the laminar range, natural-convection h depends on ΔT roughly as:
choices: \(\Delta T\) (linearly)
\(\Delta T^{1/4}\) — weakly
\(\Delta T^2\) — strongly
Not at all
quiz.solution: The Churchill–Chu form gives \(Nu\propto Ra^{1/4}\propto\Delta T^{1/4}\): h barely improves even as the surface runs much hotter.

quiz.q: As Gulf-summer ambient temperature rises (fixed surface rating), a sealed enclosure's actual rejected heat flux \(q''=h\Delta T\):
choices: Rises, since hotter air always convects better
Falls — both a shrinking ΔT and an only weakly-changing h point the same unfavorable way
Stays exactly constant, since h compensates perfectly
Is undefined once ambient exceeds surface temperature
quiz.solution: Both effects compound: h barely improves and the available ΔT shrinks, so rejected heat falls — verified numerically in this lesson's second solve problem.

quiz.q: This lesson's own computed \(h\approx4\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) compares to Lesson 4's forced-air result (\(h\approx20\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\)) as:
choices: Roughly five times higher
Roughly five times lower
Essentially identical
Natural convection is always higher than forced convection
quiz.solution: Natural convection in a gas sits near the bottom of the h ladder — a fraction of even modest forced-air values, as this lesson's comparison chart shows directly.

quiz.q: A shorter wall (smaller characteristic length L), same ΔT and fluid, has natural-convection h that is:
choices: Lower than a taller wall's — bigger surfaces always convect better
Slightly higher than a taller wall's — h weakly falls as L grows
Exactly the same regardless of L
Impossible to determine without Re
quiz.solution: Since \(h=Nu\,k/L\) and \(Nu\propto Ra^{1/4}\propto L^{3/4}\), h scales as roughly \(L^{-1/4}\) — a weak decrease with increasing L, confirmed in this lesson's third solve problem.

quiz.q: Why does Lesson 2's fin effectiveness argument apply especially strongly to natural-convection surfaces?
choices: Fin effectiveness scales with \(\sqrt{k/h}\), and natural convection's very low h is exactly the regime where that ratio — and the payoff — is largest
Fins do not work at all under natural convection
Natural convection eliminates the need for fins entirely
Fin effectiveness is independent of h
quiz.solution: Low h is precisely where \(\varepsilon_{fin}\propto\sqrt{k/h}\) is largest — exactly why finned, un-fanned enclosures are such a common design pattern.

6.kuwait:
An engineer specifying a sealed, un-fanned electrical enclosure for outdoor Gulf installation checks natural-convection h at the design ambient temperature, not at a mild reference temperature, before setting the enclosure's maximum internal dissipation rating — per §3 and §4's own numbers, a modest ambient rise can cut real cooling capacity by roughly a quarter even though h itself barely changes, so a catalog rating benchmarked at a temperate ambient overstates what the same enclosure can actually reject on a Gulf summer day.

A technician orienting a horizontal hot surface — an outdoor junction-box lid, a horizontal duct run — checks which face is up before assuming a handbook horizontal-plate h applies as written: per §2, the same \(\Delta T\) gives a meaningfully higher h with the hot face up (free plumes) than with the hot face down (stable, trapped stratification), and mixing the two up is an easy, checkable mistake.

7.lecture:
§1 · Four regimes, one curve

Plotting heat flux against excess temperature \(\Delta T_e=T_s-T_{sat}\) on log-log axes traces a shape every boiling fluid shares. Free convection boiling: too little excess temperature for bubbles yet, ordinary natural convection. Nucleate boiling: bubbles form, detach, and stir the liquid violently — \(q''\) climbs steeply for only a modest rise in \(\Delta T_e\), the most efficient and most desirable regime to operate in. The critical heat flux (CHF) is the peak of the curve. Past it, transition boiling is genuinely unstable: a partial, intermittent vapor blanket forms, and \(q''\) falls as \(\Delta T_e\) rises further. Film boiling follows: a stable vapor blanket fully covers the surface, and \(q''\) eventually climbs again at much higher \(\Delta T_e\) — but at surface temperatures that destroy most real equipment.

§2 · Zuber's CHF, and Nusselt film condensation

Zuber's correlation predicts CHF from fluid properties alone, no need to trace the whole curve up to it: \(q''_{max}=C\,h_{fg}\rho_v\!\left[\dfrac{\sigma g(\rho_l-\rho_v)}{\rho_v^2} \right]^{1/4}\), \(C\approx0.131\) — density difference, surface tension, and latent heat set the limit; the heater material does not appear at all. On the condensing side, Nusselt film theory gives a mean coefficient of the same functional form for a draining condensate film, but with a different leading constant by geometry — \(0.943\) for a vertical plate, \(0.729\) for a horizontal tube — because the controlling drainage path genuinely differs: down a vertical surface's full length versus around a horizontal tube's circumference.

§3 · Why CHF is a safety limit, and why real plants get film condensation

Many real systems are controlled by an imposed heat input — an electric heater, a fired process heater, a reactor fuel rod — not an imposed surface temperature. For such a system, an upset that pushes \(q''\) toward CHF risks trying to sit on the falling transition-boiling branch, which at a fixed \(q''\) has no stable point there at all — the system jumps almost immediately to the much higher surface temperature the film-boiling branch demands at that same \(q''\). This is literally what "burnout" means physically. Real designs therefore carry a margin below CHF, commonly operating in the 70% range of \(q''_{max}\) rather than approaching it. On the condensing side: dropwise condensation gives a coefficient often five to ten times higher than film condensation, but it requires special, often short-lived, surface treatments and is not self-sustaining on an untreated surface — which is why almost every real condenser tube runs in film condensation despite dropwise's better numbers on paper.

§4 · A worked CHF, and a design check against it

For water at 1 atm (\(h_{fg}=2257\ \text{kJ/kg}\), \(\rho_l=958\), \(\rho_v=0.6\ \text{kg/m}^3\), \(\sigma=0.0589\ \text{N/m}\)), Zuber's correlation gives \(q''_{max}=1.11\ \text{MW/m}^2\) — the same order of magnitude every heat- transfer text quotes for water at atmospheric pressure. A conservative design running at 70% of this limit operates at \(q''=0.78\ \text{MW/m}^2\); a reboiler specified instead at 0.85 MW/m² is running at \(77\%\) of CHF — above the typical 70% design range, and worth a second look before it is approved.

Key relation — Zuber critical heat flux

\[ q''_{max}=C\,h_{fg}\rho_v\left[\frac{\sigma g(\rho_l-\rho_v)}{\rho_v^2} \right]^{1/4} \]

A limit computed from fluid properties alone — no heater material, no ΔT, enters at all.

What this lesson assumes

  • Natural convection (Lesson 6) — the free-convection boiling region at the start of the curve is exactly that, before bubble dynamics take over.
  • Thermal resistance networks (Lesson 1) — boiling and condensation coefficients still plug into the same \(q=\Delta T/R\) bookkeeping used everywhere else in this course.

quiz.q:
The worked Zuber CHF

Problem. For water at 1 atm (\(h_{fg}=2257\ \text{kJ/kg}\), \(\rho_l=958\), \(\rho_v=0.6\ \text{kg/m}^3\), \(\sigma=0.0589\ \text{N/m}\), \(C=0.131\)), find the Zuber critical heat flux.
quiz.solution: Solution. \(q''_{max}=C\,h_{fg}\rho_v[\sigma g(\rho_l-\rho_v)/\rho_v^2]^{1/4}=1.11\ \text{MW/m}^2\).

quiz.q:
Checking a specified design against CHF

Problem. A reboiler for this same fluid is specified to operate at 0.85 MW/m². What fraction of the Zuber CHF is this, and is it within a typical 70% design-margin practice?
quiz.solution: Solution. \(q''_{spec}/q''_{max}=0.85/1.11=77\%\) — above the typical 70% margin range, meaning this design sits closer to the CHF cliff edge than conservative practice recommends, and is worth flagging before approval.

quiz.q:
Horizontal versus vertical condensation

Problem. For otherwise-identical fluid properties, characteristic dimension, and \(\Delta T\), compare the mean film-condensation coefficient for a horizontal tube (constant 0.729) against a vertical plate (constant 0.943), using the Nusselt constants alone.
quiz.solution: Solution. \(h_{horiz}/h_{vert}=0.729/0.943=0.77\) — the horizontal-tube arrangement's coefficient is about 77% of the vertical arrangement's, purely from the geometry-driven constant, before any difference in characteristic length is even considered.

quiz.q: Which regime of the boiling curve is the target for practical equipment design?
choices: Free convection boiling
Nucleate boiling — steep q'' rise for modest ΔT_e
Transition boiling
Film boiling
quiz.solution: Nucleate boiling delivers the largest heat flux for the smallest excess temperature — the most efficient, most controllable regime, and where real boilers are meant to operate.

quiz.q: Immediately past the critical heat flux peak, in transition boiling, what happens to \(q''\) as \(\Delta T_e\) continues to rise?
choices: It keeps rising, just more slowly
It falls — a genuinely unstable region
It stays exactly constant
It jumps discontinuously to zero
quiz.solution: Transition boiling is the falling branch of the curve — an intermittent, unstable vapor blanket actually reduces q'' as ΔT_e rises further.

quiz.q: Why is CHF treated as a safety limit rather than a performance target?
choices: Because exceeding it slightly always destroys equipment instantly, with no exceptions
Because in a heat-flux-controlled system, approaching CHF risks an uncontrolled jump to a much higher, damaging surface temperature on the film-boiling branch
Because CHF cannot be computed or predicted at all
Because CHF only matters for nuclear reactors
quiz.solution: A fixed-q'' system has no stable point on the falling transition branch; per §3, it jumps almost immediately to film boiling's much higher surface temperature — the real mechanism behind 'burnout'.

quiz.q: Zuber's CHF correlation is built from which properties?
choices: Heater material conductivity and surface roughness only
Density difference, surface tension, and latent heat of the fluid — not the heater material
Excess temperature ΔT_e alone
Reynolds and Prandtl numbers
quiz.solution: Zuber's correlation is a fluid-properties-only result — no heater material, no ΔT_e, enters the formula at all.

quiz.q: In practice, which condensation regime does a typical, untreated industrial condenser tube actually operate in?
choices: Dropwise condensation, since it is thermodynamically favored
Film condensation — dropwise needs special, often short-lived surface treatment and is not self-sustaining
Neither — real tubes never condense vapor
Whichever regime gives the lower coefficient, by design
quiz.solution: Film condensation is the overwhelming practical default; dropwise's much higher h requires surface treatments most industrial tubes do not carry.

quiz.q: The Nusselt film-condensation constant differs between a horizontal tube (0.729) and a vertical plate (0.943) primarily because:
choices: One geometry is always hotter than the other
The condensate film's controlling drainage path differs — around a horizontal circumference versus down a full vertical length
Horizontal tubes cannot condense vapor at all
The constants are arbitrary and carry no physical meaning
quiz.solution: Geometry sets how the condensate film drains, which is exactly what these leading constants encode — not an arbitrary labeling difference.

quiz.q: A reboiler specified at 0.85 MW/m² against a fluid with CHF=1.11 MW/m² is running at roughly what fraction of CHF?
choices: 77% — above a typical 60–70% design-margin range
10% — deep within a safe margin
100% — exactly at the limit
It cannot be determined from CHF alone
quiz.solution: \(0.85/1.11=77\%\), above the conservative 70% range this lesson's §3 describes as typical practice.

quiz.q: In film boiling, at very high \(\Delta T_e\), why does \(q''\) eventually rise again despite a complete, insulating vapor blanket?
choices: The vapor blanket disappears at high ΔT_e
Radiation across the vapor film becomes significant at those very high surface temperatures, adding to the reduced conduction/convection through the vapor
CHF is exceeded a second time
It does not — q'' stays flat in film boiling
quiz.solution: At the very high surface temperatures film boiling reaches, radiative transfer across the vapor film becomes a real, additive contribution, per §1's curve shape.

7.kuwait:
A process engineer specifying an electrically or fire-heated reboiler sizes its operating heat flux with a real margin below the fluid's CHF, never at it — per §3, a controlled process that briefly pushes toward CHF during an upset does not fail gracefully, it can jump almost immediately to a much higher, damaging surface temperature, which is why a design margin belongs in the original sizing calculation rather than as an afterthought once trouble starts.

A maintenance engineer evaluating a fouled or scaled condenser tube recognizes that it is virtually certainly operating in film condensation, not the far more effective dropwise regime — per §3, expecting anywhere near dropwise-level performance from an untreated industrial tube surface sets an unrealistic benchmark for what cleaning alone can restore.

8.lecture:
§1 · Architectures, and one overall coefficient

Shell-and-tube and plate exchangers both do the same job through different geometry — plates pack far more area into a given volume, shell-and-tube handles a wider range of pressures and fouling tendencies. Either way, the overall coefficient \(U\) is built exactly like Lesson 1's resistance chain: hot-side convection, tube-wall conduction, cold-side convection, in series — and in a real, aged exchanger, two more series terms as well: a fouling resistance on each side, \(R_{f,h}\) and \(R_{f,c}\), that only grow over an operating campaign.

§2 · Two design methods, and what fouling does to each

The LMTD method uses \(q=UA\,\Delta T_{lm}\), with \(\Delta T_{lm}=(\Delta T_1-\Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)\) built from the temperature gap at each end. The effectiveness–NTU method instead defines \(\varepsilon=q/q_{max}\), \(q_{max}=C_{min}(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})\), and \(NTU=UA/C_{min}\), with closed-form \(\varepsilon(NTU,C_r)\) relations for each flow arrangement — for the special case \(C_r=0\) (one stream condensing or evaporating, so its capacity rate is effectively infinite), \(\varepsilon=1-e^{-NTU}\) regardless of flow arrangement. Fouling resistance added in series lowers \(U\) directly; for a fixed area and inlet conditions, a lower \(U\) means less heat exchanged — outlet temperatures drift back toward their inlet values on both sides, not just one.

§3 · When effectiveness–NTU is the only honest method

LMTD needs the outlet temperatures to compute \(\Delta T_{lm}\) in the first place — fine for a sizing problem, where the duty and desired outlets are already specified and the question is how much area to buy. For a rating problem — a piece of hardware already exists, \(UA\) is known, and the question is what outlet temperatures it will actually deliver — the outlets are the unknown, and LMTD would need an iterative guess-and-check loop to use at all. Effectiveness–NTU solves directly from \(UA\), \(C_{min}\), \(C_{max}\), and the inlet temperatures, with no iteration — the only honest method once the outlets are what you are actually trying to find.

§4 · A worked LMTD, an NTU check, and a fouling number

A counterflow exchanger runs hot 150→90°C against cold 30→70°C. \(\Delta T_1=T_{h,in}-T_{c,out}=80\)°C, \(\Delta T_2=T_{h,out}-T_{c,in}=60\)°C, \(\Delta T_{lm}=(80-60)/\ln(80/60)=69.52\)°C. Separately, a condenser (\(C_r\approx0\)) sized to \(NTU=2\) delivers \(\varepsilon=1-e^{-2}=86.5\%\) of its maximum possible duty. And on fouling: a clean \(U_{clean}=800\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) exchanger picking up just \(R_f=0.0005\ \text{m}^2\text{K/W}\) of combined fouling resistance drops to \(U_{fouled}=1/(1/U_{clean}+R_f)=571.4\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) — a 29% loss in overall coefficient from a single, modest fouling term, exactly the kind of number a fouling-monitoring program exists to catch before it grows further.

Key relation — LMTD

\[ \Delta T_{lm}=\frac{\Delta T_1-\Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}, \qquad q=UA\,\Delta T_{lm} \]

Valid for sizing; switch to effectiveness–NTU the moment the outlets become the unknown.
