The worked LMTD

Problem. A counterflow exchanger runs hot 150→90°C against cold 30→70°C. Find \(\Delta T_1\), \(\Delta T_2\), and the LMTD.

An NTU rating check

Problem. A condenser (\(C_r\approx0\), one stream condensing) is sized to \(NTU=2\). Find its effectiveness, and the physical meaning of \(C_r\approx0\).
quiz.solution: Solution. \(C_r=C_{min}/C_{max}\approx0\) because a condensing stream's effective heat capacity rate is enormous (it changes phase at essentially constant temperature). \(\varepsilon=1-e^{-NTU}=1-e^{-2}=86.5\%\) of the maximum possible duty is delivered.

quiz.q:
A fouling-derated U

Problem. A clean exchanger has \(U_{clean}=800\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\). In service, it accumulates a combined fouling resistance of \(R_f=0.0005\ \text{m}^2\text{K/W}\). Find the fouled overall coefficient, and the percent drop from clean.
quiz.solution: Solution. \(U_{fouled}=1/(1/U_{clean}+R_f)=1/(1/800+0.0005)=571.4\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\), a \((1-571.4/800)\times100=29\%\) drop from a single, modest fouling term.

quiz.q: The overall coefficient U in a heat exchanger is best understood as:
choices: A single empirical constant unrelated to the exchanger's construction
A series-resistance network — hot film, wall, cold film, plus fouling — exactly like Lesson 1's resistance chain
Always equal to the higher of the two film coefficients
Independent of fouling by definition
quiz.solution: U is built from the same series-resistance bookkeeping introduced in Lesson 1, now with two convection films, a wall, and (in service) fouling terms.

quiz.q: The LMTD method requires which piece of information that effectiveness–NTU does not strictly need to start from?
choices: The overall coefficient U
The inlet temperatures
The outlet temperatures — needed to compute ΔT₁ and ΔT₂ in the first place
The heat exchanger's area A
quiz.solution: \(\Delta T_{lm}\) is built directly from the outlet temperatures; effectiveness–NTU instead solves for those same outlets from UA and the inlets alone.

quiz.q: A heat exchanger already installed has a known UA. Its outlet temperatures at an off-design flow rate are unknown and need to be predicted. Which method is appropriate?
choices: LMTD, solved directly with no iteration
Effectiveness–NTU — outlets are the unknown, exactly the case it is built for
Neither method applies once flow rate changes
Zuber's correlation from Lesson 7
quiz.solution: This is a rating problem (hardware fixed, outlets unknown) — the case §3 identifies as effectiveness–NTU's own, direct-solution territory.

quiz.q: Adding fouling resistance in series, area and inlet conditions held fixed, does what to U and to the heat duty q?
choices: Both increase
U decreases, and q decreases with it
U decreases but q increases to compensate
Neither changes — fouling only affects pressure drop
quiz.solution: Fouling resistance adds directly to the series network, lowering U; for fixed A and similar ΔT, that lowers q as well — verified numerically in this lesson's third solve problem.

quiz.q: For a condensing or evaporating stream, \(C_r=C_{min}/C_{max}\approx0\) because:
choices: The stream has zero mass flow rate
A phase-changing stream's effective heat capacity rate is enormous — it absorbs or releases heat at essentially constant temperature
The stream's specific heat is exactly zero
Cr is undefined for any two-phase flow
quiz.solution: Latent heat exchange at essentially one temperature makes the effective \(C\) of that stream enormous compared to the other, single-phase stream — hence \(C_r\approx0\).

quiz.q: In this lesson's condenser example, \(NTU=2\) and \(C_r\approx0\) gives an effectiveness of roughly:
choices: 100%, exactly
It cannot be found without U and A separately
quiz.solution: \(\varepsilon=1-e^{-NTU}=1-e^{-2}=86.5\%\), the direct closed form for \(C_r=0\), any flow arrangement.

quiz.q: Why is a plate exchanger often chosen over shell-and-tube for a given duty?
choices: It always tolerates more fouling and higher pressure than shell-and-tube
It packs more heat-transfer area into a given volume, at the cost of being less tolerant of high pressure or heavy fouling in many designs
It requires no U calculation at all
It cannot be used in counterflow
quiz.solution: Plates trade compactness (more area per volume) for typically lower pressure and fouling tolerance than shell-and-tube — a real design trade-off, not a strict improvement.

quiz.q: The idealized straight-line temperature profile in this lesson's own diagram is:
choices: Exact for any counterflow exchanger, regardless of flow rates
An approximation, exactly linear only when both streams' capacity rates are equal — the ΔT₁ and ΔT₂ endpoint values themselves are still exact
Only valid for parallel flow, never counterflow
Physically impossible and never used
quiz.solution: Real profiles curve according to the differential energy balance; the linear sketch is a simplification, honestly captioned as such — but the two end-gap values it starts and ends at are exact.

8.kuwait:
An engineer commissioning a new exchanger uses LMTD to confirm the specified area delivers the contracted duty at the design terminal temperatures — a sizing question with the outlets already fixed by contract. The same engineer, months later, asked what outlet temperatures the same installed hardware delivers at an off-design flow rate, switches to effectiveness–NTU without a second thought — per §3, the outlets are now the unknown, and LMTD would need to be iterated blind.

A reliability engineer tracking a fouling-monitoring program treats a modest, single fouling resistance reading as a real, quantifiable loss in overall coefficient, not a rounding error — per §4's own number, well under a millimetre-scale fouling resistance cut this lesson's example exchanger's U by over a quarter, which is exactly why fouling factors get tracked as a trended number rather than checked once at commissioning and forgotten.

9.lecture:
§1 · Blackbody laws, emissivity, and view factors

An ideal blackbody emits \(E_b=\sigma T^4\); a real gray surface emits \(E=\varepsilon\sigma T^4\), with emissivity \(\varepsilon\) (0 to 1) discounting it from the ideal. Between two surfaces, the view factor \(F_{12}\) is the fraction of radiation leaving surface 1 that lands directly on surface 2. Two bookkeeping rules make view factors tractable: reciprocity, \(A_1F_{12}=A_2F_{21}\), and the summation rule, \(\sum_j F_{1j}=1\) for a complete enclosure — every bit of radiation leaving a surface inside a closed enclosure has to land somewhere.

§2 · The network method, shields, and combined losses

For two gray surfaces exchanging radiation, the net rate is one more resistance-network calculation: \(q_{12}=\sigma(T_1^4-T_2^4)/R_{tot}\), with \(R_{tot}=\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1A_1}+\dfrac{1}{A_1F_{12}}+ \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2A_2}\) — two surface resistances bracketing one space resistance, the exact structural echo of Lesson 1's conduction chain, now driven by \(T^4\) differences instead of \(T\). Inserting a radiation shield between two surfaces adds two more surface resistances in series (one per shield face); when every surface and shield face shares the same emissivity, \(N\) identical shields divide the unshielded heat rate by exactly \(N+1\) — verified directly from the resistances themselves, not merely quoted. Combining convection and radiation losses from one surface is instead a parallel combination: linearizing radiation into an equivalent coefficient \(h_{rad}=\varepsilon\sigma(T_s+T_{surr})(T_s^2+T_{surr}^2)\) lets total loss be written as one Newton's-law-style term, \(h_{combined}=h_{conv}+h_{rad}\).

§3 · The energy-audit and thermography case

A bare, uninsulated hot pipe or vessel loses heat by convection and radiation simultaneously, and radiation is routinely the larger of the two at typical process temperatures — not a minor correction. An audit or a survey that estimates loss from convection alone, "because that's what comes to mind first," can understate the real opportunity by close to half, exactly the number §4 below computes directly rather than assumes.

§4 · A worked bare pipe, oxidized and then polished

A bare steel pipe, \(D=10\ \text{cm}\), runs at \(T_s=150\)°C in \(T_\infty=35\)°C surroundings, oxidized (\(\varepsilon=0.8\)), with an estimated \(h_{conv}=10\ \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) (Lesson 6's methods, at this modest \(\Delta T\)). \(h_{rad}=\varepsilon\sigma(T_s+T_\infty)(T_s^2+T_\infty^2)=9.09\ \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) — comparable to \(h_{conv}\) itself. \(h_{combined}=19.09\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\), \(q''=h_{combined}\Delta T=2195\ \text{W/m}^2\), or \(q'=q''\pi D=689.7\ \text{W/m}\) of bare pipe — with radiation alone carrying 48% of the total. Polishing or foil-wrapping the same pipe to \(\varepsilon=0.1\) drops \(h_{rad}\) to 1.14, radiation's share to 10%, and the total loss to \(q'=402.3\ \text{W/m}\) — a 42% cut in total heat loss from changing surface finish alone, no insulation added at all.

Key relation — two-surface radiation network

\[ q_{12}=\frac{\sigma(T_1^4-T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1A_1}+ \dfrac{1}{A_1F_{12}}+\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2A_2}} \]

The same series-resistance idea as every other lesson in this course, one more time.

What this lesson assumes

  • Thermal resistance networks (Lesson 1) — the two-surface radiation network is the same series-resistance bookkeeping, now driven by \(T^4\).
  • Natural convection (Lesson 6) — combined-loss problems need an estimated \(h_{conv}\) from exactly those methods.

TermFormulaUse
Gray-surface emission\( E=\varepsilon\sigma T^4 \)Real surface vs. ideal blackbody.
Reciprocity\( A_1F_{12}=A_2F_{21} \)View-factor bookkeeping.
Two-surface network\( q_{12}=\sigma(T_1^4-T_2^4)/R_{tot} \)Same resistance idea, T⁴ driven.

quiz.q:
The worked bare pipe, oxidized

Problem. A bare steel pipe, \(D=10\ \text{cm}\), runs at \(T_s=150\)°C in \(T_\infty=35\)°C surroundings, \(\varepsilon=0.8\), \(h_{conv}=10\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\). Find \(h_{rad}\), \(h_{combined}\), and the loss per metre of pipe.
quiz.solution: Solution. \(h_{rad}=\varepsilon\sigma(T_s+T_\infty)(T_s^2+T_\infty^2)=9.09\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\). \(h_{combined}=19.09\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\). \(q''=2195\ \text{W/m}^2\), \(q'=q''\pi D=689.7\ \text{W/m}\).

quiz.q:
The same pipe, polished

Problem. The same pipe is polished or foil-wrapped to \(\varepsilon=0.1\), all else unchanged. Find the new loss per metre, and the percent reduction from the oxidized case.
quiz.solution: Solution. \(h_{rad}=1.14\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\), \(h_{combined}=11.14\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\), \(q'=402.3\ \text{W/m}\), a \((1-402.3/689.7)\times100=42\%\) drop in total loss from surface finish alone.

quiz.q:
Radiation shields, verified from the network

Problem. Two large parallel surfaces at \(T_1=400\ \text{K}\), \(T_2=300\ \text{K}\), all surfaces \(\varepsilon=0.5\), exchange heat with no shield. Insert 3 identical (\(\varepsilon=0.5\)) shields between them. Find the ratio of shielded to unshielded heat rate, computed from the resistances directly.
quiz.solution: Solution. No shield: \(R_0=(1-0.5)/0.5+1+(1-0.5)/0.5=3\), \(q_0=\sigma(T_1^4-T_2^4)/R_0=330.75\ \text{W/m}^2\). With 3 shields, each contributing two identical surface resistances plus one more space resistance, the network totals \(R_3=4\times R_0=12\), so \(q_3=\sigma(T_1^4-T_2^4)/R_3=82.69\ \text{W/m}^2\), and \(q_3/q_0=0.25=1/(3+1)\) exactly, confirming the general \(N+1\) rule from the network itself rather than assuming it.

quiz.q: Reciprocity for view factors, \(A_1F_{12}=A_2F_{21}\), expresses which physical fact?
choices: Both surfaces must have equal emissivity
The radiation exchange between two surfaces is symmetric in a specific, area-weighted sense, regardless of which surface is labeled 1 or 2
View factors are always equal to 1
Only true for blackbodies
quiz.solution: Reciprocity is a geometric bookkeeping identity, independent of emissivity — it holds for any two surfaces' view factors regardless of material.

quiz.q: The summation rule \(\sum_j F_{1j}=1\) applies to which situation?
choices: Any two surfaces in open space
A surface inside a complete, closed enclosure — every bit of leaving radiation must land somewhere
Only blackbody surfaces
Only surfaces of equal area
quiz.solution: Inside a closed enclosure, radiation leaving a surface has nowhere to go but onto the other enclosure surfaces (including possibly itself), summing to exactly 1.

quiz.q: Combining convection and radiation losses from one surface, using \(h_{combined}=h_{conv}+h_{rad}\), is structurally which kind of combination?
choices: Series resistances, like Lesson 1's conduction chain
Parallel conductances — two independent paths for the same driving ΔT
Neither — the two modes cannot be combined at all
A weighted average, not a sum
quiz.solution: Both modes act on the same surface and the same ΔT simultaneously, in parallel — adding their coefficients directly, unlike the series addition used for layers encountered one after another.

quiz.q: N identical radiation shields (same emissivity as the two surfaces) inserted between them divide the unshielded heat rate by:
choices: N
2N
It is unaffected by the number of shields
quiz.solution: Each shield adds one more identical resistance unit to the series network; N identical shields give N+1 identical resistance units total, dividing q by N+1 — verified directly from the resistances in this lesson's third solve problem.

quiz.q: In this lesson's bare-pipe example, radiation carries roughly what share of the total loss from the oxidized (\(\varepsilon=0.8\)) surface?
choices: About 48% — comparable to convection, not a minor correction
About 5% — negligible next to convection
100% — convection contributes nothing
It cannot be estimated without a full enclosure view-factor calculation
quiz.solution: \(h_{rad}/h_{combined}=48\%\) — genuinely comparable to convection's share, exactly the field-relevant point §3 makes.

quiz.q: Polishing or foil-wrapping the same bare pipe surface (lowering ε) primarily reduces loss through which mechanism?
choices: Convection — polishing changes the boundary layer
Radiation — h_rad is directly proportional to ε, so a lower ε cuts it almost proportionally
Conduction through the pipe wall
It has no effect on either mode
quiz.solution: \(h_{rad}\propto\varepsilon\) directly; lowering emissivity cuts radiative loss sharply while leaving convection essentially untouched, as this lesson's second solve problem shows numerically.

quiz.q: An energy audit tallying only convective loss from a bare, uninsulated hot pipe risks:
choices: Overstating the loss by roughly double
Understating the real loss by close to half, since radiation is routinely comparable to convection at these temperatures
No error at all — radiation is always negligible for pipes
An error only relevant above 500°C
quiz.solution: This lesson's own worked numbers show radiation carrying nearly half the total loss — an audit ignoring it misses a comparable-sized opportunity, not a rounding error.

quiz.q: Two identical surfaces exchange radiation. Doubling the absolute temperature of the hotter surface (surroundings fixed) changes the net radiative exchange by:
choices: Doubling it
Roughly a factor of up to sixteen, since the driving term is \(T_1^4-T_2^4\), not \(T_1-T_2\)
No change, since ε is unaffected
Halving it
quiz.solution: The fourth-power dependence in \(T_1^4-T_2^4\) makes radiative exchange far more sensitive to absolute temperature than any linear rate law — doubling \(T_1\) can raise \(T_1^4\) sixteen-fold on its own.

9.kuwait:
A facilities engineer surveying bare, uninsulated hot piping tallies both convection and radiation loss, not convection alone — per §4's own numbers, radiation can carry close to half of a bare hot pipe's total loss at typical process temperatures, and an audit that counts convection only understates the real energy-saving opportunity from insulating by nearly double.

A reliability engineer specifying a radiation shield between a hot process component and nearby instrumentation sizes the number of shields from the network method, not a guess — per §2, each identical shield divides the unshielded heat rate by exactly one more, a specific number worth checking against the actual protection required rather than assuming "a shield" is enough regardless of how many are fitted.

10.lecture:
§1 · One mathematics, two names

Fick's law of diffusion, \(N''=-D_{AB}\,dC/dx\), has exactly the shape of Fourier's law, \(q''=-k\,dT/dx\) — mass diffusing down a concentration gradient the same way heat diffuses down a temperature gradient. Convective mass transfer carries the same structure forward too: a convective mass transfer coefficient \(h_m\) plays the role \(h\) plays in Newton's law, \(N''=h_m(C_s-C_\infty)\) standing in for \(q''=h(T_s-T_\infty)\).

§2 · The heat–mass analogy, and why it works so well for air and water specifically

The Chilton–Colburn analogy connects the two convection coefficients, \(h/h_m\approx\rho c_p\,Le^{2/3}\), where the Lewis number \(Le=\alpha/D_{AB}\) compares thermal diffusivity to mass diffusivity. For water vapor diffusing in air specifically, \(D_{AB}\approx2.5\times10^{-5}\ \text{m}^2\text{/s}\) happens to sit very close to air's own thermal diffusivity — a genuine physical coincidence for this particular pair of substances, not a universal law — giving \(Le\approx1\) and the simplified Lewis relation \(h/h_m\approx\rho c_p\). That coincidence is exactly why wet-bulb thermometry and simple evaporative-cooling estimates work as well, and as simply, as they do for air-water problems, and why the same shortcut should not be assumed for an arbitrary pair of substances.

§3 · Why evaporation cools what is left behind

Vaporizing a mass of liquid costs its latent heat of vaporization. With no external heat source feeding that cost, the energy has to come from somewhere — and the only reservoir on hand is the sensible heat of the liquid (and immediate surroundings) left behind, which cools as a direct result. This is the entire physical mechanism behind a wet-bulb thermometer, evaporative cooling pads, and the cooling felt from wet skin in moving air — not a separate "evaporation is cold" fact, but the same energy balance as every other lesson in this course, applied to a phase change instead of a temperature difference.

§4 · A worked evaporation estimate, reusing this course's own convection numbers

Lesson 4's flat-plate result gave \(h=20\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) for forced air at a modest speed. Using the Lewis relation with \(\rho_{air}=1.15\ \ \text{kg/m}^3\), \(c_{p,air}=1007\ \text{J/kgK}\): \(h_m=h/(\rho c_p)=0.0173\ \text{m/s}\). Taking a representative (given, not a claimed precise psychrometric lookup) vapor-density difference between a wet surface and hot, dry Gulf air, \(\Delta\rho_v=0.018\ \text{kg/m}^3\): \(N''=h_m\Delta\rho_v=3.11e-04\ \text{kg/m}^2\text{s}=1.12\ \ \text{kg/m}^2\text{hr}\) — a real, order-of-magnitude-plausible desert evaporation rate. Converted through \(h_{fg}\approx2.45\ \text{MJ/kg}\), this is \(q_{evap}=N''h_{fg}=763\ \text{W/m}^2\) of cooling drawn from the surface left behind — a substantial number, on the same order as this course's other worked convective fluxes, and the same "sensible heat pays for the latent heat" bookkeeping §3 describes.

Key relation — the Lewis relation, air-water only

\[ \frac{h}{h_m}\approx\rho c_p \qquad (Le\approx1\text{ for water vapor in air}) \]

A shortcut earned by a specific numerical coincidence — check \(Le\) before reusing it for any other fluid pair.

  • Psychrometrics (Thermodynamics II) — the sensible/latent energy split this lesson's evaporative cooling relies on was introduced there for cooling-load estimates.

quiz.q:
The worked evaporation estimate

Problem. Reusing Lesson 4's \(h=20\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\), with \(\rho_{air}=1.15\ \text{kg/m}^3\), \(c_{p,air}=1007\ \text{J/kgK}\), and a representative \(\Delta\rho_v=0.018\ \text{kg/m}^3\), find \(h_m\), the evaporation flux \(N''\), and the equivalent cooling flux \(q_{evap}\) (\(h_{fg}\approx2.45\ \text{MJ/kg}\)).
quiz.solution: Solution. \(h_m=h/(\rho c_p)=0.0173\ \text{m/s}\). \(N''=h_m\Delta\rho_v=3.11e-04\ \text{kg/m}^2\text{s}\). \(q_{evap}=N''h_{fg}=763\ \text{W/m}^2\).

quiz.q:
The same estimate at Lesson 6's much lower h

Problem. Repeat the estimate using Lesson 6's natural-convection result instead, \(h=3.99\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\), same air properties and \(\Delta\rho_v\). Find the new \(h_m\), \(N''\), and \(q_{evap}\).
quiz.solution: Solution. \(h_m=h/(\rho c_p)=0.00345\ \text{m/s}\). \(N''=h_m\Delta\rho_v=6.20e-05\ \text{kg/m}^2\text{s}\). \(q_{evap}=N''h_{fg}=152\ \text{W/m}^2\) — about \(5\times\) smaller than the forced-air case, since $h_m$ scales directly with the (much lower) natural-convection h.

quiz.q:
Mass evaporated from a real surface

Problem. At §4's flux, how much water evaporates from a 2 m² open surface over 1 hour?
quiz.solution: Solution. \(m=N''\times A\times t=3.11e-04\times2\times3600=2.24\ \text{kg}\) over the hour.

quiz.q: Fick's law and Fourier's law share which structural relationship?
choices: They are unrelated equations that happen to look similar by coincidence
Both state that a flux is proportional to minus a property times a concentration or temperature gradient — the same mathematical form, different transported quantity
Fick's law only applies to gases, Fourier's law only to solids
Fick's law replaces Fourier's law whenever radiation is present
quiz.solution: Both are linear diffusion laws of identical form, \(-(\text{property})\times(\text{gradient})\) — heat and mass diffuse by the same mathematics.

quiz.q: The Lewis number \(Le=\alpha/D_{AB}\) compares which two quantities?
choices: Momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity
Thermal diffusivity to mass diffusivity
Convective to conductive heat transfer
Buoyancy force to viscous force
quiz.solution: Le compares how fast heat diffuses (α) to how fast mass diffuses (\(D_{AB}\)) — when the two rates are similar, \(Le\approx1\).

quiz.q: The simplified Lewis relation \(h/h_m\approx\rho c_p\) is a reliable shortcut specifically because:
choices: It holds for any two substances, by definition
For water vapor in air, \(D_{AB}\) happens to be numerically close to air's thermal diffusivity, giving \(Le\approx1\) — a specific coincidence, not a universal law
Air has zero viscosity
Water always evaporates at a fixed rate
quiz.solution: This is a property coincidence specific to the air-water pair; §2 is explicit that it should not be assumed to transfer to other fluid combinations.

quiz.q: Physically, why does evaporation cool the liquid left behind, with no external heat source present?
choices: It does not — evaporation has no thermal effect on the remaining liquid
The latent heat needed to vaporize the escaping mass is drawn from the remaining liquid's own sensible heat, cooling it
The remaining liquid gains heat from the vapor that just left
Evaporation only cools the surrounding air, never the liquid
quiz.solution: With no external heat input, the energy balance has nowhere else to draw the latent heat from except the sensible heat of what remains — this is the literal mechanism, not a figure of speech.

quiz.q: In this lesson's worked estimate, moving from Lesson 4's forced-air \(h=20\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) to Lesson 6's natural-convection \(h\approx4\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) changes the evaporation flux by:
choices: No change — evaporation is independent of h
It falls by roughly the same factor h itself fell, since \(h_m\propto h\) via the Lewis relation
It rises, since lower h means less convective cooling competition
It becomes negative
quiz.solution: \(h_m=h/(\rho c_p)\) is directly proportional to h; a lower h gives a proportionally lower \(h_m\) and evaporation flux, confirmed in this lesson's second solve problem.

quiz.q: A wet-bulb thermometer reads a lower temperature than a dry-bulb thermometer in the same air primarily because:
choices: Wet-bulb thermometers are less accurate
Evaporation from the wet wick draws latent heat from the wick's own sensible heat, cooling it below the surrounding dry-bulb air temperature
Water is inherently colder than air
The wick blocks radiation to the thermometer
quiz.solution: This is exactly §3's mechanism in its most common instrument form — the wick cools itself by evaporating into the surrounding air.

quiz.q: A representative vapor-density difference \(\Delta\rho_v\) used in a worked example should be presented as:
choices: A precise psychrometric-chart value recalled exactly from memory
A given, representative input value — honestly labeled as such, not claimed as a precise lookup
Irrelevant — any value produces the same q_evap
Always exactly zero for hot, dry air
quiz.solution: This course's standing practice (also used for steam-table values in Thermodynamics II) is to present such figures as honest, representative GIVEN inputs, never as claimed precise recalled lookups.

quiz.q: This lesson's evaporation estimate reused convection coefficients from which two earlier lessons?
choices: Lesson 1 and Lesson 2
Lesson 4 (forced convection) and Lesson 6 (natural convection)
Lesson 7 and Lesson 8
No earlier lesson's numbers were reused
quiz.solution: The worked example and its second solve problem explicitly reuse Lesson 4's forced-air h and Lesson 6's natural-convection h, tying the analogy back to numbers already computed and verified earlier in this course.

10.kuwait:
An engineer estimating water loss from an open process-water surface or a cooling pond in Gulf summer heat uses the Lewis relation to convert an already-known or already-estimated h into \(h_m\), rather than deriving a mass-transfer correlation from scratch — per §4, this shortcut is earned specifically by \(Le\approx1\) for air and water vapor, and should not be assumed to carry over unchanged to a different fluid pair.

A technician noticing that a wetted evaporative cooling pad, or curing concrete kept damp, stays noticeably cooler than the surrounding dry air is watching the same mechanism as a wet-bulb thermometer — per §3, the energy leaving as vapor is drawn directly from the remaining liquid's own sensible heat, a real, quantifiable effect rather than a vague "evaporation feels cool" impression.

11.lecture:
§1 · Framing the brief: sizing, then margining

This course's capstone is a synthesis of Lesson 8: given a duty (a process stream's flow rate, properties, and required temperature change) and a coolant or heating stream, size a shell-and- tube exchanger to meet it, then carry a real fouling margin on top — because the duty and desired outlets are specified in advance, this is squarely a sizing problem, and the LMTD method from Lesson 8 is the right tool to reach for, not effectiveness–NTU.

§2 · The full sizing chain

The process stream's duty follows directly from its own energy balance, \(Q=\dot m_h c_{p,h}(T_{h,in}-T_{h,out})\); the coolant's required flow or outlet temperature follows from the same energy balance applied to it, \(Q=\dot m_c c_{p,c}(T_{c,out}-T_{c,in})\). With all four terminal temperatures now known, \(\Delta T_{lm}\) builds exactly as in Lesson 8, and a clean-service area follows from \(A_{clean}=Q/(U_{clean}\Delta T_{lm})\), using a representative clean-\(U\) figure for the service (a given design input, not a fabricated precision). A fouling margin is then spent one of two mathematically equivalent ways: buy extra area, \(A_{design}=A_{clean}\times(1+\text{margin})\), or keep the area fixed and instead derate the design \(U\) by the same factor, \(U_{design}=U_{clean}/(1+\text{margin})\) — the same engineering decision, expressed two different ways on two different spec sheets.

§3 · Critiquing the design against how it actually fails

Real fouling is not a one-time step to the design margin and then a flat line — it accumulates over an operating campaign, and the first symptom of it outrunning the design margin is a duty shortfall at unchanged flow: outlet temperatures drift back toward their inlet values, long before anything resembling a dramatic failure. At that point the practical options are a flow increase (which pushes back against the rest of the piping system's own curve, a cost this lesson's numbers do not include) or a cleaning outage. The fixed-percentage margin itself is a simplification worth stating plainly, not defending as exact — real fouling rates are time- and service-dependent, which is exactly why a trended duty calculation, not a one-time design check, is what actually catches the problem in the field. If any part of a real service could locally vaporize, Lesson 7's CHF caution becomes a second, independent failure mode to check — fouling margin does not protect against that at all; and an un-insulated unit runs Lesson 9's bare-surface loss on top of everything else.

§4 · The worked capstone

Hot oil, \(\dot m_h=4\ \text{kg/s}\), \(c_{p,h}=2100\ \ \text{J/kgK}\), cools 95→60°C: \(Q=\dot m_h c_{p,h}\Delta T=294\ \text{kW}\). Cooling water, \(\dot m_c=3\ \text{kg/s}\), \(c_{p,c}=4180\ \text{J/kgK}\), enters at \(T_{c,in}=25\)°C and must leave at \(T_{c,out}=T_{c,in}+Q/(\dot m_c c_{p,c})=48.4\)°C to absorb the same duty. Counterflow: \(\Delta T_1=46.56\)°C, \(\Delta T_2=35\)°C, \(\Delta T_{lm}=40.50\)°C. At a representative clean \(U_{clean}=450\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) for this liquid-liquid service, \(A_{clean}=Q/(U_{clean}\Delta T_{lm})=16.13\ \text{m}^2\). Carrying a 20% fouling margin, \(A_{design}=19.36\ \text{m}^2\) — equivalently, the same margin as a derated \(U_{design}=U_{clean}/1.2=375 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) at the 19.36 m² area, exactly as §2 describes: two spec sheets, one design decision.

Key relation — sizing with a fouling margin

\[ A_{design}=A_{clean}(1+m)=\frac{Q}{U_{clean}\,\Delta T_{lm}}(1+m) =\frac{Q}{[U_{clean}/(1+m)]\,\Delta T_{lm}} \]

Extra area and a derated design U are the same margin, algebraically, every time.
11.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • The LMTD sizing method (Lesson 8) — this capstone is its direct applied extension, start to finish, with a fouling margin added at the end.

Toolkit — the capstone sizing chain

TermFormulaUse
Duty\( Q=\dot m c_p\Delta T \)From either stream's own energy balance.
Clean area\( A_{clean}=Q/(U_{clean}\Delta T_{lm}) \)Sizing to the specified duty.

quiz.q:
The full capstone sizing chain

Problem. Hot oil, \(\dot m_h=4\ \text{kg/s}\), \(c_{p,h}=2100\ \text{J/kgK}\), cools 95→60°C. Cooling water, \(\dot m_c=3\ \text{kg/s}\), \(c_{p,c}=4180\ \text{J/kgK}\), enters at \(T_{c,in}=25\)°C. Find \(Q\), \(T_{c,out}\), and the LMTD.

Sizing the area, and its fouling-margin equivalent

Problem. At a representative clean \(U_{clean}=450\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\), find \(A_{clean}\), then the design area at a 20% fouling margin, and the equivalent derated design U at that same design area.
quiz.solution: Solution. \(A_{clean}=Q/(U_{clean}\Delta T_{lm})=16.13\ \text{m}^2\). \(A_{design}=A_{clean}\times1.2=19.36\ \text{m}^2\). \(U_{design}=U_{clean}/1.2=375\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\) — the same margin, restated as a coefficient instead of an area.

quiz.q:
A tighter margin

Problem. A reviewer proposes a leaner 10% fouling margin instead of 20%. Find the resulting design area and equivalent design U, and compare both to the 20%-margin case.
quiz.solution: Solution. \(A_{design}'=A_{clean}\times1.1=17.74\ \text{m}^2\), smaller than the 20%-margin's 19.36 m². \(U_{design}'=U_{clean}/1.1=409.1\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}\), higher than the 20%-margin's 375 — a leaner margin buys a smaller, cheaper exchanger today at the cost of a shorter run before fouling erodes the smaller cushion.

quiz.q: This capstone's sizing problem is a sizing problem rather than a rating problem because:
choices: The overall coefficient U is unknown
The duty and desired terminal temperatures are specified in advance, and the question is how much area to buy — exactly Lesson 8 §3's sizing case
Rating problems never involve terminal temperatures
Effectiveness–NTU cannot be used for liquid-liquid service
quiz.solution: Per Lesson 8, sizing problems start from known duty and outlets and solve for area; that is exactly this capstone's brief.

quiz.q: Expressing a fouling margin as extra installed area versus as a derated design U are:
choices: Two unrelated design choices, only one of which is ever correct
The same engineering decision, algebraically equivalent, restated two different ways
Only equivalent if the exchanger is counterflow
Only equivalent when the fouling margin is exactly 50%
quiz.solution: \(A_{design}=A_{clean}(1+m)\) and \(U_{design}=U_{clean}/(1+m)\) at that same \(A_{design}\) describe the identical design decision, shown algebraically in §2.

quiz.q: As a real exchanger fouls in service beyond its design margin, the FIRST symptom is typically:
choices: Sudden mechanical failure of the tubes with no warning
A duty shortfall at unchanged flow — outlet temperatures drifting back toward inlet values
An immediate, unplanned shutdown with no intermediate stage
No observable symptom until the unit is opened for inspection
quiz.solution: Per §3, a duty shortfall at otherwise-normal flow is the earliest, most easily trended warning sign — well before anything catastrophic.

quiz.q: The fixed-percentage fouling margin used in this lesson's sizing chain should be understood as:
choices: An exact physical law with no uncertainty
A practical simplification — real fouling accumulates in a time- and service-dependent way, not as a fixed percentage
Unnecessary once a good U value is known
Only relevant for gas-side services, never liquid-liquid
quiz.solution: §3 is explicit that the fixed-percentage margin is a simplification, not a physical law — which is exactly why a trended duty check matters in the field.

quiz.q: If part of this capstone's process stream could locally vaporize in service, which earlier lesson's failure-mode caution becomes independently relevant?
choices: Lesson 6's natural-convection orientation effect
Lesson 7's critical-heat-flux safety margin — a separate risk fouling margin does not protect against at all
Lesson 3's Biot-number check
Lesson 10's Lewis-relation restriction
quiz.solution: CHF is a distinct failure mode from fouling; per §3, a fouling margin on area does nothing to protect against a local boiling excursion toward CHF.

quiz.q: In this lesson's own numbers, tightening the fouling margin from 20% to 10% changes the design area and equivalent design U how?
choices: Smaller design area (17.7 vs 19.4 m²), higher equivalent U (409 vs 375 W/m²K)
Larger design area, lower equivalent U
No change to either quantity
Smaller area but also lower equivalent U
quiz.solution: A leaner margin buys a smaller 17.74 m² unit at a higher 409.1 W/m²K equivalent design U — cheaper today, less cushion against future fouling.

quiz.q: An un-insulated capstone exchanger shell adds which additional, independent loss on top of the sizing calculation itself?
choices: None — the sizing calculation already accounts for all losses
Lesson 9's combined convection-radiation bare-surface loss from the shell to its surroundings
A second fouling resistance identical to the tube-side one
A correction to the LMTD itself
quiz.solution: An un-insulated shell loses heat to its surroundings by the same convection-plus-radiation mechanism Lesson 9 quantifies — a separate loss from the sizing calculation's own internal duty.

quiz.q: Which single earlier lesson is this capstone most directly a synthesis and extension of, start to finish?
choices: Lesson 3 (transient conduction)
Lesson 8 (heat exchangers: LMTD and effectiveness–NTU)
Lesson 6 (natural convection)
Lesson 10 (mass transfer)
quiz.solution: Every step of this capstone — duty, terminal temperatures, LMTD, area, margin — is Lesson 8's own sizing method carried through a complete worked design.

11.kuwait:
A design engineer sizing a new liquid-liquid cooler carries an explicit fouling margin — extra area, or equivalently a derated design U — rather than sizing to the clean-service number and hoping in-service performance matches it; per §2, an engineer comparing two vendor spec sheets should first check which of the two equivalent conventions each one is quoting before comparing their numbers directly.

A maintenance engineer reading a declining-duty trend on an in-service liquid-liquid exchanger recognizes an outlet temperature drifting back toward its inlet value, at otherwise unchanged flow, as the first symptom of fouling outrunning its design margin — per §3, this is exactly the kind of early, quantifiable warning a trended duty calculation is meant to catch well before a forced, unplanned cleaning outage becomes the only option left.
